Following the trial court's dismissal of the case, the pre-emptor, who was both the appellant and petitioner, took the matter to the District Judge of Kushtia, after which it was transferred to the Additional District Judge. In the appellate court, the pre-emptor submitted a petition to convert his case, but this was rejected by the Additional District Judge. The pre-emptor then lodged a Civil Revision with the High Court Division (HCD), which made the rule absolute. Subsequently, the pre-emptee, unhappy with this, filed a Civil Petition for Leave to Appeal with the Appellate Division and was granted leave, leading to the present appeal. Ultimately, the Appellate Division allowed the appeal.
statutes_cited:
Md. Rabiul Islam and others Vs. Sultan Mahmud died leaving behind his heirs: (1) 
                                Md. Abu Hasnat (Bulbul)and others (Md. Nuruzzaman, 
                                  J) 15SCOB[2021] AD 95 Key Words: Pre-emption; Section 96 of the State 
                                  Acquisition and Tenancy Act, 1950; Section 24 
                                  of the Non-Agricultural Tenancy Act, 1949
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 15 SCOB [2021] AD 6] rejected

facts_summary: The central issue before the court was whether a pre-emptory right persists in the hands of a pre-emptor after a co-sharer to whom a pre-emptee transferred their holding subsequently assigns the property to another co-sharer. In reviewing the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act's sections 90 and 96, the Appellate Division considered relevant precedents, namely 50 C.W.N. 806 and 35 DLR 238, and took note of an earlier ruling in 35 DLR (AD) 225. Ultimately, the Appellate Division ruled that even in the event of a transfer to a non-party by the original transferee, a co-sharer pre-emptor's pre-emptory right remains unextinguished.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] AD 10] manual

facts_summary: The Government faced a setback when their petition was filed late, 403 days after the Appellate Court's judgment and decree, which had awarded a water body recorded in the Khas Khatian to the respondents. This tardiness led to the High Court Division refusing to waive the delay and discharged the petition. The Government then appealed the High Court Division's ruling. The Appellate Division ruled that the Government's delay resulted from the prolonged completion of official procedures, which was beyond their control, and did not constitute an excessive delay that warranted dismissal. Consequently, the Appellate Division set aside the High Court Division's decision and permitted the Government to waive the delay.
statutes_cited:
Govt. 
                                of Bangladesh & ors Vs. Md. Abdul Jalil & ors (Md. Abu Zafor Siddique, 
                                  J) 17 SCOB [2023] AD 74 Key Words: Section 5 of Limitation Act, 1908; Condonation 
                                  of delay; delay made by the government; Section 
                                  115(1) of the Code of Civil Procedure
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] AD 7] manual

facts_summary: The property in question belonged to Rukkhini Dashi, who had acquired it using her own earnings. Upon Rukkhini's demise, her only daughter Hazari Sundory Dashi inherited the property. The latter's only child, the plaintiff Elokeshi Mondol, succeeded Hazari Sundory Dashi. However, defendants 1-6, paternal uncles of the plaintiff, managed to record the property in their names, and when they denied the plaintiff's title, she filed a lawsuit to assert her claim. The trial court ruled in the plaintiff's favor, a decision upheld by the appellate court. The High Court Division subsequently confirmed the judgments, establishing that the property was indeed part of Rukkhini Dashi's own earnings. Defendants 1-6 appealed to the Appellate Division, arguing that under the 'Dayabhaga' system, properties inherited by women, regardless of their origin, do not become their personal property, but rather grant a limited interest. Upon the property owner's death, it passes not to their heirs but to the next available heir of the original owner. This principle, they claimed, invalidated the impugned judgments. Conversely, the plaintiff contended that as a daughter, she inherits the property absolutely, just as a son would, due to equal inheritance rights, thereby acquiring full authority to dispose of the property as she sees fit. The Appellate Division, having reviewed relevant texts from 'The Dayabhaga' by Jimuta Vahana, Mulla's 'Principles of Hindu Law' and hearing the opinion of an amicus curiae, ultimately accepted the plaintiff's argument and dismissed the defendants' appeal, thereby affirming the lower court's decision.
statutes_cited:
Mrigangka 
                                Mohan Dhali & ors Vs. Chitta Ranjan Mondol & ors (Borhanuddin, 
                                  J) 18 SCOB [2023] AD 20 Key Words: The Hindu Law of Inheritance (Amendment) Act, 
                                  1929; Dayabhaga Law of Inheritance; Stridhan; 
                                  doctrine of religious efficacy; limited interest
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 3] affirmed

facts_summary: The Appellate Division of the High Court overturned a previous decision, ruling that the High Court Division had erred in its judgment. This was based on an examination of sections 2(7), 3, 9, 10, 11, and 13 of the 2010 Act, which indicated that the High Court Division had acted contrary to the statute by allowing the writ petitioner to extract sand from the Meghna River bed in the specified Mouzas. The Government had initially filed a leave petition against the High Court Division's order, which had directed the authorities to collaborate with the writ petitioner in extracting 86.30 lac cubic meters of sand/earth. The writ petitioner was to cover the costs of this extraction using a country-made dredger for the purpose of maintaining navigability of the river. The Appellate Division consequently directed the Deputy Commissioner of Chandpur to take measures to collect the royalty for the sand that had already been extracted from the writ petitioner.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                & ors Vs. Md. Selim Khan & ors (M. Enayetur Rahim, 
                                  J) 18 SCOB [2023] AD 36 Key Words: Article 102 of the Constitution; Sections 2 
                                  (7), 3, 9, 10, 11 and 13 of বালুমহাল 
                                  ও মাটি ব্যবস্থাপনা আইন ২০১০ ; Balumahal; 
                                  mandamus; Ports Act, 1908 and Ports Rules, 1966
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 4] manual

facts_summary: The subject matters of these appeals and civil petitions revolved around several critical determinations. Specifically, the courts were compelled to examine whether the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission (BTRC) is authorized to impose an additional spectrum fee based on the Market Competition Factor (MCF) and if such a measure is lawful. Additionally, the judicial body had to decide whether a cellular mobile phone operator is permitted to withhold Value-Added Tax (VAT) collected at source and then remit it directly to the government's treasury, as well as whether the VAT paid by these operators on spectrum fees and license fees can be claimed as a rebate. Furthermore, it had to be determined whether the BTRC is obliged to compulsorily register under the VAT Act. Dissenting from some prior findings of the High Court Division, the Appellate Division, after a thorough review of relevant laws, rules, and guidelines, reached the following conclusions: Firstly, in conformity with clause-12(viii) of the Cellular Mobile Phone Operator Regulatory and Licensing Guidelines, 2011, the BTRC retains the right to alter or introduce new charges or levies as needed, and the mobile operators are duty-bound to adhere to such decisions. Consequently, the imposition of an additional spectrum fee based on the MCF is lawful. Secondly, the cellular mobile phone operators are not permitted to withhold VAT collected at source; instead, the BTRC must collect VAT from the operators and then deposit it into the government's treasury. Moreover, the VAT incurred by the operators on spectrum fees and license fees cannot be claimed as a rebate. Thirdly, according to clause-7 (Ab¨vb¨ †mev)(N) of the second schedule of the VAT Act, 1991, the BTRC is not required to obtain compulsory VAT registration.
statutes_cited:
Grameenphone 
                                Ltd & ors Vs. BTRC & Ors (Borhanuddin, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 96 Key Words: Sub-Section 3(Ga) of Section 3, 5(4), Sub-Section 
                                  4 of Section 6, Section 6 (4KaKa), Section 9(Uma), 
                                  15 (4) of the VAT Act, 1991; Rule 18(Ka) and 
                                  18(Uma) of the VAT Rules, 1991; Cellular Mobile 
                                  Phone Operator Regulatory and Licensing Guidelines, 
                                  2011; Market Competition Factor (MCF);
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] AD 4] manual

facts_summary:
The plaintiffs, jointly with respondent No.2, commenced Title Suit No. 199 of 2001 in the 1st Court of Assistant Judge, Dhaka, seeking judicial validation that the application of the Conservancy and Pilotage Service Fees Rules, 1990, as enacted under the Merchants Shipping Ordinance, 1983, to the plaintiffs' Coastal Ships and Tankers was unlawful and requesting a declaration that certain letters and a circular dated June 17 and 25, 2001, and circular No. M- 2/15/47(4) were null and void. The trial Court rejected their plea. 

The defendants subsequently lodged an appeal, which was successful, and the appellate Court overturned the trial Court's verdict. However, the defendants then pursued Civil Revision in the High Court Division and secured a Rule, which was upheld by a Single Bench. Consequently, the plaintiffs filed a civil petition for leave to appeal to the Appellate Division, which granted leave, thus resulting in the present appeal, which was ultimately dismissed.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                  TailbahiJahaj Malik Samity Vs. Chairman, 
                                  BIWTA (Md. Ashfaqul Islam, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: নৌসংরক্ষণওপরিচালনএবংপথনির্দেশনাফিসবিধিমালা, 
                                  ১৯৯০; Merchant Shipping Ordinance, 1983; Definition of Inland Ship; Imposition of Conservancy fees upon Inland Ship 
                                  according to Bidhimala,1990; Double Jeopardy;
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 2] rule absolute

facts_summary: Four civil appeals, denoted as Nos. 171–174 of 2015, derived from a High Court Division ruling of 13.02.2014, which ruled that certain land acquisition proceedings in Chittagong were unlawful. The crux of the disputes revolved around Land Acquisition Case No.1/2010-2011, wherein the Ministry of Land had authorized the acquisition of lands for a power project in the absence of the alleged mandatory clearance from the Prime Minister. The writ petitioners, comprising a multitude of companies and individuals, maintained that they possessed legitimate leases or ownership of the lands and that their objections to the acquisition were deliberately disregarded. They based their claims on a handwritten note purportedly signed by the then Prime Minister on 14.11.2010, directing the Ministry to submit a revised report, which was never fulfilled. Consequently, the High Court declared that the memo issued by the Ministry on 23.11.2010 lacked lawful authority, was malicious, and unconstitutional, as the Prime Minister's sanction was indispensable. The Deputy Commissioner of Chittagong appealed, contending that the Prime Minister's note was fabricated and that the High Court had relied on forged documents. The Appellate Division found merit in this assertion, remarking that the writ petitioners had referenced confidential government files without proper authorization. The Court underscored that fraud inherently nullifies all legal proceedings and that no relief can be granted to litigants who approach the court with tainted hands. It ruled that the alleged note of 14.11.2010 was nonexistent and fabricated, thereby invalidating the foundation of the High Court's decision. Ultimately, the Appellate Division set aside the High Court's judgment and allowed all four civil appeals, thereby reinstating the government's acquisition process.
statutes_cited:
Deputy 
                                  Commissioner, Chittagong Vs. Interbulk 
                                  Overseas SA Bangladesh Limited and another. (Farah Mahbub, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Settlement case, The Acquisition and Requisition 
                                  of Immovable Property Ordinance, 1982, L.A. 
                                  Case, Lease agreement, clean hands, acquisition 
                                  process, fraud upon the Court,
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 7] declared

facts_summary: A public interest suit was lodged by ASK, asserting the eviction of slum dwellers from government-owned land, citing infringement of fundamental freedoms. The government responded by asserting that the property was allocated for the construction of a government office and that the applicants were unauthorised occupants lacking a legitimate claim to housing.
statutes_cited: Government and Local Authority Lands and Buildings (Recovery of Possession) Ordinance 1970 Section 5(1); Constitution Articles 27, 31, 32
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 2] Rule discharged; right to shelter is non-justiciable and unlawful occupation creates no legitimate expectation against eviction

facts_summary: In a lawsuit, claimants requested judicial confirmation of their property rights to land that had previously submerged due to river erosion in 1967 but resurfaced in 1974. The State countered by asserting that, pursuant to Section 86 of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act as revised in 1972, the property had automatically transferred to the State since it reemerged after the prescribed deadline.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 86; P.O. No. 135 of 1972
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 14] Appeal dismissed; land reappearing after 1972 vests absolutely in Government; plaintiffs' rights were extinguished

facts_summary: A group of plaintiffs contested the decision of a District Judge in a matter related to a title execution, where the court allowed a delay and permitted amendments but chose not to suspend temporarily the execution proceedings. The plaintiffs contended that the failure to grant a temporary halt equated to a tacit refusal.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure Section 115(4)
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2024] HCD 15] Rule made absolute; non-grant of interim stay constitutes implied refusal entitling petitioners to seek revisional remedy

facts_summary: A lawsuit was initiated by the claimant to enforce a contractual obligation to reacquire a parcel of land. This right was alleged to arise from a deed conveying ownership with a stipulation that granted the claimant an option to repurchase the property until 1971, as specified in the agreement dated 1963. However, the statutory provision P.O. No. 88 of 1972 took effect at a time when the claimant's repurchase option had already lapsed, thereby rendering the transaction final and complete.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Sections 95 and 95A; P.O. No. 88 of 1972; P.O. No. 136 of 1972
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 4] Plaintiff not entitled to relief as repurchase condition expired before Presidential Order; transaction was past and closed

facts_summary: A litigant initiated a judicial proceeding to divide jointly held real property, asserting an interest in the estate. The defendants countered by disputing the plaintiff's ownership. The judicial tribunal ruled that when a defendant contests the plaintiff's title in the course of a partition action, the court must first investigate and establish the validity of the plaintiff's claim before directing the division of the property.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure Section 54; Order 20 Rule 18; Order 26 Rule 13
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 5] Remanded for proper adjudication of title and partition together to avoid multiplicity of proceedings

appellant: East West Property Development (Pvt.) Ltd. and another
facts_summary: The applicant contested the revocation of a property transfer document by the Deputy Commissioner, which was prompted by a land acquisition proceeding that had become invalid due to the revocation of the applicable legislation. A formal government order confirming the legitimate acquisition of the land had not been issued at the time the proceeding lapsed.
statutes_cited: Emergency Requisition of Property Act 1948; স্থাবর সম্পত্তি অধিগ্রহণ ও হুকুমদখল আইন ২০১৭
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 13 SCOB [2020] HCD 19] Property acquisition found invalid; no government decision existed and acquisition proceeding became non-est due to statutory repeal

appellant: Monto Sheikh being dead his legal heirs
respondent: Ibrahim Miah being dead his legal heirs
facts_summary: The plaintiff asserted dominion and control over the subject property, yet failed to demonstrate a legitimate claim to ownership. The plaintiff's grounds for possession rested on a settlement that had been effectively superseded, the cancellation of which remained valid and in effect.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872; Code of Civil Procedure
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 258] Plaintiff failed to prove title; cancelled settlement cannot form valid basis of title claim

respondent: Charge Officer and Appeal Officer and others
facts_summary: A group of petitioners brought a challenge against a Settlement Officer who utilized their authority under Rule 42 to effectively erase all land record proceedings that had reached the Appellate Division, thereby altering the natural progression prescribed by the foundational legislation. The Government had failed to grant the Settlement Officer the power to undo the previous stages of the parent law's process.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Rules 1955 Rule 42; Settlement Officer jurisdiction
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 234] Settlement Officer's nullification of parent law proceedings declared a nullity; revenue officers cannot reverse what Parliament prescribed

facts_summary: In a dispute over a revoked land lease, the petitioners contested the authority's actions, arguing that the cancellation of the lease preceded any opportunity for them to present their case. In accordance with the fundamental concept of natural justice, a decision should precede, not follow, a hearing where all relevant facts can be presented.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; audi alteram partem
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 9 SCOB [2017] HCD 11] Authority violated natural justice by cancelling lease before affording petitioners opportunity to be heard; hearing must be pre-decisional

facts_summary: A pre-emption dispute unfolded, with the pre-emptee asserting entitlement under Section 96(10) of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act, basing their claim on familial ties. The court scrutinized the eligibility of a brother-in-law for protection under the Succession Act's definition of relatives within three degrees of consanguinity.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96(10); Succession Act 1925 Section 28
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 7 SCOB [2016] HCD 64] Pre-emptee not entitled to protection as brother-in-law is not within three degrees of consanguinity; pre-emption protection denied

facts_summary: In a dispute over ownership rights, the defendant occupants resisted eviction by the claimant property owner, who challenged the validity of their interest in the land. The legal issue at hand revolved around whether the defendant's denial of the owner's title necessitated compliance with Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act prior to effecting their removal from the premises.
statutes_cited: Transfer of Property Act Section 106
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 96] Defendants forfeited right to stay by denying landlord's title; notice under Section 106 dispensed with when tenant denies title

facts_summary: The plaintiff sought to assert ownership over a plot of land that had been submerged underwater prior to 1965 and originally classified as government khas land, which was then recorded in the official government records. The defendant, however, contended that the submerged land had vested in them under Section 86 of the State Acquisition of Tenancy (SAT) Act. The court undertook an analysis of the legal implications arising from the land's submergence and the government's entitlement to lease out the land that had re-emerged.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 86; P.O. No. 135 of 1972
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 4 SCOB [2015] HCD 52] Government acquired lawful right to lease diluvated land; plaintiff's title extinguished after diluvion regardless of prior ownership

facts_summary: A lawsuit pertaining to land ownership originated within the Chittagong Hill Tracts, focusing on the validity of a deed and the consequences of a mutation case. The court scrutinized the necessity of issuing formal notifications to designated statutory bodies, as mandated by the Chittagong Hill Tracts Regulation of 1900, prior to commencing litigation. Furthermore, the court investigated the enforceability of customary laws prevailing in the Chittagong Hill Tracts.
statutes_cited: Chittagong Hill Tracts Regulation 1900; Registration Act 1908; Constitution Article 152
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 14] Case establishes mandatory notice requirement in CHT land suits; outcome for specific parties not determinable from available ratio

facts_summary: In a contentious dispute over land ownership, the claimant pursued a permanent injunction to protect their property rights. The defendant's assertion of title rested on City Corporation tax receipts, but a critical examination revealed a lack of a genuine chain of title. The court ultimately characterized the defendant's claim as an illicit land acquisition, with their deeds failing to explicitly reference a specific parcel of land.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; property title and chain of ownership
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 13 SCOB [2020] HCD 9] City Corporation tax receipt alone not proof of possession; land grabber's claim rejected as isolated from any valid chain of title

facts_summary: A plaintiff petitioned against a court's decision to deny their request to set aside a complaint due to the defence of res judicata. The disputed land was formally designated as a forest reserve through a government Gazette in 1952, followed by its acquisition by the State under the provisions of the State Acquisition and Tenancy Act. Subsequent actions by the Forest Department to further classify the land were deemed superfluous by the Court.
statutes_cited: Forest Act 1927; State Acquisition and Tenancy Act Sections 3(2) and 20(2a)(iii)
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 49] Rule discharged; Gazette notification declaring land as forest sufficient to determine character without additional Forest Act declaration

facts_summary: In a pre-emption dispute, the pre-emptors brought a case alleging that they were not informed of the sale of the land, purportedly to their detriment. However, testimony revealed that prior to the transaction, the vendors had initiated negotiations with the pre-emptors with the intention of selling the land, but the pre-emptors declined the offer and instead recommended that the vendors sell the property to the individual who ultimately acquired it. This sequence of events effectively extinguished the pre-emptors' pre-emption rights.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 96; Registration Act 1908 Section 60; Non-Agricultural Tenancy Act 1949 Section 24
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 284] Rule discharged; pre-emptors waived pre-emption right through their conduct before and after the sale

facts_summary: The Chittagong Port Authority opposed a lawsuit lodged against it, citing a failure on the part of the opposing party to provide prior notice under the terms of Section 49 of the Chittagong Port Ordinance 1976. In its defense, the Port Authority contended that the lawsuit was ill-founded due to the lack of notice being served. Ultimately, the court ruled that once the matter had been placed before it, the complaint could not be dismissed and that the service of summons had effectively met the prerequisite of notice.
statutes_cited: Chittagong Port Ordinance 1976 Section 49; Code of Civil Procedure Order VII Rule 2
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 34] Rule discharged; Port Authority's challenge failed; service of summons with plaint equivalent to required notice where no alternative remedy exists for land disputes

facts_summary: A lawsuit was initiated by the plaintiffs seeking a judicial declaration that a purportedly registered sale deed was indeed a fabrication, based on the contention that the document was not publicly recited to the illiterate parties prior to its execution. The lower court ultimately granted the plaintiffs' claim, but this decision was subsequently overturned by a higher appellate tribunal. Ultimately, the High Court ruled that the plaintiffs had not successfully met their evidentiary obligation to refute the legally established presumption that the registration of the document was valid.
statutes_cited: Registration Act 1908 Sections 31, 32, 34, 35; Evidence Act 1872 Sections 101, 103; Specific Relief Act Section 42
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 93] Rule discharged; registered deed carries strong presumptive evidence of valid execution; plaintiff failed to rebut presumption

appellant: Probir Kumar Dey alias Saiful and another
facts_summary: Following the demise of the landowner, a dispute arose over the division of 4.81 decimals of land. The Hindu widow contested that she and her son were entitled to an equal share in the property under the Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act, 1937. However, their claim was complicated by the fact that one of the sons had converted to Islam prior to the institution of the partition suit. The court consequently had to investigate and balance the rights of the Hindu widow, the converted son, and their unmarried daughter in relation to the distribution of the inheritance and the partition of the land.
statutes_cited: Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act 1937 Section 3; Caste Disability Removal Act 1850; Dayabhaga school of Hindu Law
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 154] Plaintiffs entitled to partition; widow has same rights as son; converted son lost right to father's property; unmarried daughter entitled to property

facts_summary: A lawsuit was initiated by the plaintiffs seeking to establish their rightful ownership of a property, based on allegations that certain vital documents had been tampered with. It was contended that these defendants, due to the incapacitation of their father who was confined to his bed, blind, and incapacitated from 1980 until his passing, must have falsified the disputed deeds bearing his signature. However, defendants disputed these claims. Initially, the trial court ruled in favor of the plaintiffs but this decision was later overturned by a higher appellate court.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Sections 101, 103; Registration Act 1908; Limitation Act 1908 Article 120
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 199] Suit set aside; plaintiff failed to discharge burden of proof; suit also barred by limitation as cause of action arose earlier than claimed

facts_summary: A rural couple brought a claim seeking to have a Nadabinama deed set aside, alleging their signatures were procured under false pretenses. The deed, they contended, was misrepresented to them as a partition agreement, when in fact it may have conveyed a different interest in the property. Given the couple's limited literacy, it is claimed they were vulnerable and susceptible to deception by the literate defendants.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Section 102; Limitation Act 1908 Section 18; Registration Act 1908
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 37] Rule discharged upholding deed cancellation; reading out a deed to illiterate executants before execution is a custom having force of law

facts_summary: Buyers of RAJUK land parcels acquired legal title via court-enforced sale proceedings validated by the Appellate Division. Subsequently, the Anti-Corruption Commission served notices to these individuals, claiming they had intentionally avoided paying registration fees. The petitioners contested the validity of these notices, arguing that the commission's actions were motivated by bad faith due to the judicial confirmation of the auction sale.
statutes_cited: Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2004 Sections 19, 20; ACC Rules 2007 Rules 3(5) and 20; Registration Act 1908 Section 63A
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 70] ACC notices quashed; evasion of registration fees for court-confirmed auction sale does not constitute scheduled offence under ACC Act; notices issued mala fide

facts_summary: The appellants contested the incorporation of their landholdings into the 1986 Gazette as having been abandoned. Documentation demonstrated that the government never secured physical control over the property, accepted land taxes from the appellants continuously until 2015, and the Rajdhani Unnayan Khalifat (RAJUK) had issued building permits. Notwithstanding, the government was unable to produce any records of possession or upkeep, prerequisites for classifying the land as abandoned, as stipulated by relevant statutes.
statutes_cited: Abandoned Buildings (Supplementary Provisions) Ordinance 1985 Section 5(1)(a); P.O. 16 of 1972
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 21] Rule made absolute; property not abandoned as Government never took possession; mandatory precondition of possession not satisfied

facts_summary: A civil appeal stemming from a dispute regarding a 7 decimal land title was brought before the appellate court for review. The appellate court scrutinized the pertinent oral and documentary evidence, including a relevant CS khatian and other supporting records, in order to make an informed determination. The High Court then evaluated whether the lower court had properly analyzed the documentary evidence in accordance with the provisions of the Evidence Act.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Sections 65 and 115; Civil Rules and Orders Chapter 17
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 27] Appeal dismissed; trial court decree for plaintiff upheld; documentary evidence properly assessed including CS khatian accepted as exhibit

facts_summary: In 1991, the claimant acquired a plot of land and retained possession until it was forcibly taken away by the respondent, a subsequent buyer, in 1997. Initially, the trial court issued a partial judgment in the claimant's favor, but the First Appellate Court overturned this decision, ruling that a partition petition had not been submitted. However, the High Court Division reinstated the trial court's decision. Ultimately, the Appellate Division determined that since prior possession and dispossession had indeed been proven, a request for partition was not a prerequisite.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; Transfer of Property Act; title and possession
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 5] Appeal dismissed; plaintiff who proved title, prior possession and dispossession can sue for recovery without also praying for partition

facts_summary: A prior appellate court judgment established that a contentious RAJUK plot did not belong to respondents claiming it as part of their CS and SA plot and had not been officially released from government acquisition. Notwithstanding this determination, the respondent launched a new lawsuit challenging title to the plot. The applicant then submitted a writ petition requesting transfer of possession and execution of a lease agreement. The High Court dismissed the writ application, but the appellate court reversed this decision, ruling that the High Court's action contravened Article 111 of the constitution, as the respondent's title claim was foreclosed by the principle of res judicata.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 111; res judicata; land acquisition proceedings
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] AD 1] High Court Division committed illegality in discharging the writ; respondent's title claim over disputed plot already decided against him in earlier proceedings; barred by res judicata

respondent: Lakkatura Tea Company Ltd and others
facts_summary: A lawsuit initiated by the plaintiff was met with an adverse ruling, predicated upon the provisions of Section 52A of the Registration Act and Section 53C of the Transfer of Property Act. The error lay in the failure to include the plaintiff's name in the Record of Rights. The court, in its deliberations, established that the Record of Rights does not serve as a definitive proof of title but possesses a certain degree of presumptive value. Consequently, an individual who has been mistakenly omitted from the Record of Rights is empowered to seek a declaratory judgment from the court.
statutes_cited: Registration Act Section 52A; Transfer of Property Act Section 53C; Code of Civil Procedure Order VII Rule 11
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 11 SCOB [2019] HCD 50] Plaint rejection overturned; plaintiff made out a distinct cause that should be adjudicated; inherent jurisdiction cannot be invoked to reject the plaint at inception

facts_summary: A co-ownership dispute centred on the disputed property resulted in litigation between the co-owners. The defendants were in physical possession and exercised control over the disputed land in question. The trial court intervened by appointing a custodian, a receiver, under Article 40, Rule 1 of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC) to safeguard the disputed property until a final decision could be made. The defendants challenged the appointment of the receiver and appealed this decision.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure 1908 Order 40 Rule 1; partition suit provisions
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 9 SCOB [2017] HCD 167] Receiver appointment upheld; prima facie danger to property established; defendants in possession might alienate mesne-profits and deprive plaintiff sisters of their rights pending final decree

facts_summary: This case involved a dispute over land ownership with competing claims of title. One party presented a registered kabala deed dated 13 May 1965, while the opposing party relied on the entries in the record of rights. The court investigated the weight of registered deeds as evidence compared to record of rights entries in establishing land ownership rights.
statutes_cited: Evidence Act 1872 Section 90; Registration Act 1908
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 29] Registered kabala prevails over record of rights; registered deed over 30 years old from proper custody presumed genuine under Section 90 of Evidence Act; record of right alone does not confer title

respondent: Chairman, 1st Court of Settlement and another
facts_summary: Pursuant to the Land Ordinance 16 of 1972, the land in question was deemed relinquished. Notwithstanding, the petitioners' entitlement to the property was predicated upon a purchase agreement. However, their vendor was not in actual possession of the land at the time the property was declared relinquished. The petitioners did not acquire possession until 1984, following which they brought a suit for specific performance of the contract of sale. Nevertheless, the petitioners contested the designation of the property as relinquished.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Abandoned Property Order P.O. 16 of 1972 Article 7; Abandoned Buildings Supplementary Provision Ordinance 1985 Section 5
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 93] Abandoned property designation stands; petitioners not in possession at relevant time; decree for specific performance does not determine the abandoned character of property

facts_summary: A partition action was initiated with certain legal heirs remaining unjoined as parties to the suit. The applicants claimed they would be disadvantaged if these unjoined heirs were not formally brought into the proceedings. The court considered whether the rights of unjoined legal heirs could still be safeguarded in the event of a partition of the property in question.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure 1908 Order 40 Rule 1; partition suit provisions
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 147] Non-impleaded legal heirs not prejudiced; as heirs they are entitled to shares of their predecessors; even non-contesting parties can pray for allotment of saham on payment of court fees before drawing up final decree

facts_summary: A litigant initiated a case in the civil court against the Settlement Officer's decision rendered during the revisional survey conducted under Section 144 of the State Acquisition of Land Act. However, the court determined that the statutory bar in Section 144B of the same Act prohibits the filing of any civil court suits up to the point of final publication of the record-of-rights.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Section 144B; Tenancy Rules Rule 42A
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 7 SCOB [2016] HCD 134] Civil court suit not maintainable during revisional survey period before final publication of record-of-rights; only remedy is Rule 42A of the Tenancy Rules

facts_summary: A higher court reversed a lower court's decision that a registered gift deed was invalid due to a lack of delivery of possession. The registered deed, executed between the donor and donee, was challenged in the High Court, which sought to address the legal efficacy of a Hindu gift deed despite the absence of physical possession transfer.
statutes_cited: Transfer of Property Act 1882 Section 123; Hindu gift law principles
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 33] Appellate court's finding reversed; where gift instrument has been registered, delivery of possession is not essential for validity of a gift by a Hindu

facts_summary: The petitioner's property was unilaterally listed as abandoned by the government without prior notification to the owner or lawful occupant under the provisions of Article 7, P.O. 16 of 1972, and Section 5(1)(b) of the aforementioned Ordinance 1985. Crucially, no summons or directive was issued to compel the petitioner to demonstrate justification for retaining possession of or vacating the premises.
statutes_cited: Abandoned Buildings (Supplementary Provisions) Ordinance 1985 Section 5(1)(b); P.O. 16 of 1972 Article 7; natural justice
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 67] Property not legally abandoned; non-service of mandatory notice disentitles government from claiming legal abandonment; petitioner's right to natural justice violated

facts_summary: The plaintiffs brought an action for the invalidation of a deed, arguing that it had been improperly executed due to the lack of knowledge by plaintiff number three, who was a minor at the time, and that no record of the deed was made at the relevant registrar's office. Furthermore, the plaintiffs contended that they were deceived into signing the deed, as the contents were not read or explained to them before their signatures were obtained. Instead, they were led to believe it was a document related to the amicable division of their respective shares in the property in question. The trial court initially rejected the plaintiffs' claim, but the appellate court overturned this decision. Consequently, the defendant, seeking to reverse this outcome, filed a civil revision with the High Court, which ultimately upheld the appellate court's findings that the deed was not properly read to the plaintiffs prior to signing, citing a local custom that requires documents to be read to the signatories. The High Court also agreed that the appellate court's determinations regarding the applicable time limits and burden of proof were correct, and thus, it discharged the rule.
statutes_cited:
Abedun 
                                Nessa Vs. Jaher Sheikh and others (Md. Rezaul Hasan, J) 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 37 Key Words: Cancellation of a deed; Requirements in case 
                                  of execution of a deed; Section 102 of the Evidence 
                                  Act; Custom; Section 18 of the Limitation Act, 
                                  1908
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 4] reversed

facts_summary:
The first respondent, as claimant, initiated a lawsuit for partition, citing unpaid dowry rights stemming from the marriage contract, specifically the nikahnama, in which her father-in-law transferred a 0.09-acre parcel of land to serve as dowry for her husband. The lower court ultimately ruled in her favour, allocating a share of the aforementioned 0.09-acre land to her. However, upon appeal, the High Court Division scrutinized whether a father-in-law could legally transfer land to act as dowry or a portion thereof, as stipulated in Clause 16 of the marriage contract, under Islamic law and applicable legislation.

In evaluating the provisions of the Family Courts Ordinance 1985, the Muslim Family Laws Ordinance 1961, the Registration Act 1908, and the Transfer of Property Act 1882, and taking into account the views of the amicus curiae, the High Court Division determined that the property in question was indeed a portion of the dowry to be transferred in the claimant's favour. The father-in-law was found to be entitled to transfer the land in lieu of a specific portion of the dowry under Islamic law; however, such a transfer was deemed ineffectual due to the provisions of sections 17A and 17B of the Registration Act 1908.

As a result, the only viable course of action for the claimant was to file a suit for dowry in the Family Court. The High Court Division consequently overturned the lower court's decision but granted the claimant permission to withdraw the appeal and refile the lawsuit in the Family Court, as established under the Family Courts Ordinance 1985.
statutes_cited:
Sadrul 
                                Huq being dead his heirs & ors. Vs. Farhana Firdousi & anr (Sheikh Hassan Arif, J) 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 62 Key Words: Family Court Ordinance 1985; The Muslim Family 
                                  Laws Ordinance 1961: Section 17A and 17B of 
                                  Registration Act 1908; Transfer of Property 
                                  of Act 1882; dower
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 6] manual

facts_summary: A matter of Civil Revision emerged concerning the scope of authority exercised by a District Judge in a case before the court. Anamika Corporation Ltd. initiated arbitration proceedings under the Arbitration Act 2001 by filing an Arbitration Miscellaneous Case with the District Judge, seeking an interim order to safeguard the scheduled property until the completion of arbitration proceedings. The opposite parties disputed the existence of an agreement and subsequently filed an application under the Evidence Act 1872, seeking the examination of their signatures by a handwriting expert. The District Judge partially allowed this application, prompting Anamika Corporation Ltd. to seek revision. The High Court Division found that the power to order the examination of a signature by a handwriting expert was delegated to the Arbitral Tribunal under the Arbitration Act 2001, specifically section 17(ka). Consequently, the District Judge was not authorized to issue an order under the Evidence Act 1872, section 45, while granting ad-interim restraint or injunction. As the Civil Court was restricted to exercising its powers under Section 7K of the Act in urgent situations, the High Court Division ruled in favor of Anamika Corporation Ltd., making the Civil Revision rule absolute.
statutes_cited:
Anamika 
                                Corp. Ltd. & ors Vs. Humayun M. Chowdhury & ors (Kashefa Hussain, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 119 Key Words: Order 7 Rule 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure,1908 
                                  ; Section 45 of the Evidence Act, 1872 ; Section 
                                  2, 7A, 10, 17(ka), 19(1)(4), and 32 of the Arbitration 
                                  Act, 2001; Expert opinion; Valid arbitration 
                                  agreement; Ad-interim injunction
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 10] manual

facts_summary: In the present case, the central issue is whether a court action can proceed against the Chittagong Port Authority without initial notification as required by Section 49 of the Chittagong Port Ordinance, 1976. Additionally, it is also pertinent to determine whether the court can subsequently address the matter of the action's maintainability despite the failure to provide prior notice. The High Court Division ruled that once the court has accepted the case and concluded its hearing, it cannot refuse the plaint. Furthermore, the Court decided that, given the absence of an alternative remedy under the Chittagong Port Ordinance, 1976, in disputes over land between the authority and a private individual, serving the summons along with a copy of the plaint on the authority shall be deemed sufficient. Consequently, the High Court Division released the rule.
statutes_cited:
Chattogram 
                                Port Authority Vs. Md. Mehedi Hasan (Md. Ruhul Quddus, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 34 Key Words: Section 49 of the Chittagong Port Ordinance,1976; 
                                  Order VII, Rule 2;Order XIV, Rule 2; Order XV, 
                                  Rule 3 and Section 151 of the Code Of Civil 
                                  Procedure; Rejection of plaint, Service of Notice
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 4] rejected

facts_summary:
Rabindra Kumar Dey held ownership and possession of 4.81 decimals of land when he passed away in 1978. His widow, Arati Bala Dey, and their two sons, Prodip and Probir, along with their four daughters, survived him. Unfortunately, Prodip predeceased his father, leaving behind his sibling Probir, who subsequently converted to Islam. Arati Bala Dey initiated a partition suit, invoking her rights under the Hindu Women's Rights to Property Act 1937, specifically claiming a share (saham). However, her demise during the lawsuit led to her daughter Shipra Rani Dey being substituted as the plaintiff.

The High Court Division was required to determine, under the Daya Bhaga school of Hindu law, whether Arati Bala Dey had inherited her deceased husband's property, and if Shipra Rani Dey was entitled to inheritance from her deceased father and mother. Furthermore, the court needed to assess whether the plaintiffs were eligible to obtain a decree for partition as sought. Upon evaluating relevant statutes, including the Caste Disability Removal Act 1850 and the Bangladesh Laws (Revision and Declaration) Act 1973, the High Court determined that in the event of a Hindu dying intestate and owning property, his widow would emerge as the sole proprietor and co-sharer during her lifetime, holding a position equivalent to that of a son in matters of partition claims.

Notably, the court decided that Probir's conversion to Islam resulted in him relinquishing his right to his father's property. Consequently, Shipra Rani Dey, Rabindra Kumar Dey's unmarried daughter, was entitled to the property on partition.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 12] manual

facts_summary: A former Additional District Judge, having served nineteen years as a Judicial Officer, challenged the constitutionality of Rule 300(a) of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I, in a Writ Petition filed at the High Court Division. The petitioner had submitted his resignation and subsequently applied for pension and other benefits, which were approved by the Ministry of Law, Justice and Parliamentary Affairs. Nevertheless, the Comptroller and Auditor General of Bangladesh disputed the entitlement to pension, citing Rule 300(a) of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I, as the relevant provision. In response, the High Court Division exercised its Writ jurisdiction to determine the validity of the contested rule. The High Court Division held that Rule 300(a) of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I, is unconstitutional to the extent that it subjects an employee with an unblemished service record, who has resigned, to the same treatment as an employee who has been dismissed due to misconduct. The Court found that an employee with an impeccable service record cannot be equated to one who has been found guilty of some wrongdoing and subsequently dismissed from service. However, the Court ruled that the remaining parts of Rule 300(a) and Rule 300(b) are valid and constitutional.
statutes_cited:
Md 
                                Mahboob Murshed Vs. Bangladesh & ors (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 7 Key Words: Article 102 of the Constitution of the People’s 
                                  Republic of Bangladesh; ultra vires; Rule 300 
                                  of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I; the 
                                  due process; resignation; forfeiture; doctrine 
                                  of severability
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 2] declared

facts_summary: A procurement solicitation was issued by Bangladesh Railway for the conception, creation, delivery, installation, activation, operation, upkeep, and technology transfer of its online-based Integrated Ticketing System. SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN JV, a collaborative venture, took part in the solicitation. The Tender Evaluation Committee (TEC) identified five tenderers, including the petitioner and respondent No. 9, as technically qualified. Subsequently, after reviewing the financial proposals of the five technically qualified tenderers, the TEC declared the petitioner as the final qualified tenderer. Consequently, a notification of award was issued. Meanwhile, respondent No. 9 submitted a complaint with the relevant authority under Rule 57(1) and (2) of the Public Procurement Rules, 2008, alleging improper and unlawful evaluation of tenders by the TEC. Later, respondent No. 9 lodged a complaint with Review Panel-2 under Rule 57(12) of the same Rules. The petitioner, as well as the relevant respondents, appeared and contested the complaint filed by respondent No. 9. However, following a hearing of the opposing parties, Review Panel-2 allowed the Review Petition and recommended re-tendering. The petitioner appealed the decision of Review Panel-2 to the High Court Division. The High Court Division ruled that respondent No. 9 did not submit the complaint within the timeframe prescribed by law, rendering it barred by limitation. Additionally, it found that Review Panel-2 failed to provide any findings regarding the limitation period in its decision, rendering it unenforceable.
statutes_cited:
SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN 
                                JV Vs. CPTU & ors (Farah Mahbub, J) 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 25 Key Words: Article 102 of the Constitution of the People’s 
                                  Republic of Bangladesh ; Rules 8, 36(3) 56, 
                                  57, 60, 98, 102 of the Public Procurement Rules, 
                                  2008; Sections 29, 30 of the Public Procurement 
                                  Act, 2006
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 3] declared

facts_summary: In a recent High Court case, the paternal grandparents of two minor children initiated a writ petition after the untimely death of their son, the children's father. The grandparents requested that the High Court ensure the minors were not being held in their mother's custody without lawful authority. The mother contested the writ petition, revealing that a separate lawsuit for custody of the minors was ongoing in the Family Court. The Family Court had issued multiple orders granting the grandparents visitation rights to the minors. However, the grandparents claimed the mother disregarded these court-ordered visitations, prompting them to file the writ petition. A High Court Division hearing with the minors concluded that they had a close relationship with their mother and an icy relationship with the grandparents. Consequently, the High Court held that the welfare of the minors should take precedence in custody decisions, ruling that the minors' welfare would be best served by remaining in their mother's custody until the Family Court suit could be resolved. Nevertheless, the grandparents were permitted to visit the children at the mother's residence upon mutual agreement and to meet them at any time and place with no binding obligation on the mother. The High Court also directed the Family Court to expedite its trial proceedings in the family suit.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 9] manual

facts_summary: The applicant, a successful bidder at an auction for a seized vessel, sought relief from the High Court Division. He requested that the Marshall of the Court be instructed to transfer the vessel to him without imposing customs duties or Value Added Tax (VAT). The plaintiff relied on an earlier statement from the Assistant Commissioner of Customs in Chattogram, which allegedly indicated that no customs duties were applicable to the vessel. He further argued that since the vessel was ordered to be sold as scrap, a Bill of Entry was necessary for customs duties to be claimed, and therefore the customs department was estopped from demanding payment. However, the High Court Division disagreed, examining provisions of the Customs Act, specifically sections 18, 23, 43, 44, 45, 51, 52, 53, and 79, as well as relevant sections of the Import and Export Act. The court concluded that any foreign vessel entering Bangladesh, regardless of whether a Bill of Entry is submitted, is subject to customs duties. Consequently, the court dismissed the applicant's request, directing him to accept delivery of the vessel only upon payment of the requisite customs duties and other government charges.
statutes_cited:
Chattogram 
                                Dry Dock Ltd Vs. M.T. Fadl-E-Rabbi & ors (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 82 Key Words: Estoppel; Customs Duty; Bill of Entry; Imported 
                                  goods; Section 115 of the Evidence Act, 1872; 
                                  Section 18, 23, 43, 44, 45, 51, 52, 53 and 79 
                                  of the Customs Act, 1969; Section 2(c), 3(1) 
                                  and 3(2) of the Imports and Exports (Control) 
                                  Act, 1950
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 7] rule is discharged

facts_summary: The petitioner contested the CEO of Biman Bangladesh Airlines Ltd's decision to terminate his employment due to alleged improper motives. In response, the respondent argued that the CEO possesses the authority to issue such directives. The respondent further posited that Section 114 of the Evidence Act, particularly illustration (e), implies that official actions are taken lawfully and in accordance with procedure. It was claimed that the petitioner failed to dispute this assumption. Nonetheless, the High Court Division assessed the relevant statutes and examined the available evidence, determining that for terminating an employee's position, a resolution from the Biman Bangladesh Airlines board of directors is mandatory. The lack of such a resolution, coupled with the absence of delegated authority, rendered the CEO's order null and void, arbitrary, and tainted by malice.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                Mominul Islam Vs. Bangladesh & ors (Zafar Ahmed, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 108 Key Words: Rule 5(Ka) of “h¡wm¡­cn ¢hj¡e L­f¡Ñ­lne LjÑQ¡l£ 
                                  (Ahpli¡a¡ J A¡e¤­a¡¢oL) ¢h¢dj¡m¡, 1988; Bangladesh 
                                  Biman Corporation Ordinance, 1977; principle 
                                  of approbation and reprobation ; Section 114 
                                  of the Evidence Act, 1872
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 9] manual

facts_summary:
The Respondent, an Inspector of Labor, conducted an inspection of the GTC and discovered alleged infractions under the Bangladesh Labor Act, 2006. The Inspector submitted a complaint to the third Labor Court in Dhaka, citing three primary breaches of labor laws: (i) the failure to convert labor and employee positions to permanent status after completing probationary periods, (ii) the denial of annual leave with pay or the option to cash out leave or receive monetary compensation for annual leave, and (iii) the non-establishment of a Labor Participation Fund and a Labor Welfare Fund, as well as the non-deposit of 5% of the company's net profit into the Sramik Kollan Foundation, as mandated by the Sramik Kollan Foundation Act, 2006.

The Petitioner argued that the case was barred by the six-month statute of limitations outlined in Section 314 of the Bangladesh Labor Act, 2006, as there was no record of the alleged incidents occurring within the specified timeframe. They also contended that even if the allegations were true, they could not be linked to the Petitioner, who was an internationally recognized individual with no involvement in the company's financial or administrative affairs. Furthermore, the Petitioner asserted that, as a nonprofit organization registered under Section 28 of the Companies Act, 1991, the GTC was exempt from establishing a Labor Participation Fund, and that its employees were appointed on a contractual basis due to its work in the telecommunication sector under contract with other companies.

The High Court Division, reviewing the relevant laws, rules, and admitted facts, rejected the Petitioner's contentions, holding that the limitation issue is a mixed question of law and fact that cannot be determined without considering evidence. Additionally, the Court found that Section 28 of the Companies Act, 1991, did not exempt any company from contributing to the Labor Welfare Fund, and that the Memorandum and Articles of Association for the GTC stated that the Board of Directors exercised full managerial and financial control over the company, making it responsible for its management and administration. As a result, the Court determined that it could not be concluded at this stage of the proceedings that the Petitioner had no role in the financial management and administration of the GTC, and consequently, the Rule was discharged.
statutes_cited:
Prof. 
                                Muhammad Yunus Vs. The State (SM Kuddus Zaman, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 162 Key Words: Sections 303 (Uma), 307, 117, 314 of Bangladesh 
                                  Labor Act, 2006; Labor Welfare Foundation Law, 
                                  2006; Section 28 of the Companies Act; Bangladesh 
                                  Labor Rules, 2015
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 13] manual

facts_summary:
A loan totaling 250,000 taka had been defaulted, prompting the initiation of a certificate case against the petitioner, who was subsequently ordered by the Certificate Officer to repay a monthly sum of 5,000 taka commencing on February 5, 2008. Subsequently, the Certificate Officer instructed the debtor to repay the loan amount in various installments, which the debtor duly complied with. However, on February 1, 2016, the Certificate Officer sought clarification from the certificate holder regarding the outstanding balance of the debtor's certificate, only to be informed that a sum of 507,766 taka was still owed.

Against this backdrop, the petitioner challenged the validity of the certificate proceedings in the High Court Division, obtaining a Rule and stay in the process. The High Court Division determined that, under Section 5(5) of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003, the certificate proceedings did not suffer from the jurisdictional defects raised by the petitioner. Nonetheless, the Court found that the Certificate Officer initiated the proceedings without conducting a requisite assessment and merely on the basis of an improperly submitted requisition letter, while also failing to establish that the entire claimed outstanding balance was indeed due at the relevant time. Consequently, the High Court quashed the certificate proceedings.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                Shahin Ikbal Vs. General Certificate Officer & ors (Md. Zakir Hossain, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 168 Key Words: Section 4, 6, 16 of the Public Demands Recovery 
                                  Act, 1913; Section 5(5) of the Artha Rin Adalat 
                                  Ain, 2003; Certificate proceeding; Certificate 
                                  Officer; Writ of certiorari; Calculation of 
                                  interest
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 14] quashed

facts_summary: These writ petitions were brought by individuals who received disciplinary actions from the University after being accused of engaging in hazing activities. A central issue in these cases centered around whether the petitioners were afforded a fair opportunity to present their side of the story prior to the imposition of penalties. Furthermore, the petitioners disputed that the university authority's actions were lawful and compliant with applicable university regulations. The High Court Division ultimately determined that the petitioners did indeed receive ample opportunity for a hearing and that the authority acted appropriately under the university's disciplinary code. Although the court agreed that multiple incidents occurred, the petitioners' argument that they were being punished multiple times for the same offense was deemed unfounded. In light of the petitioners' youth, the court decided to lessen the severity of the penalties imposed.
statutes_cited:
A.S.M. 
                                Mahadi Hassan & ors Vs. BUET & ors (J.B.M. Hassan, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 33 Key Words: Ragging, Section 4, 5, 17, 21 and 24 of the 
                                  Ordinance relating to the Board of Residence 
                                  and Discipline;
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 4] manual

facts_summary:
The dispute in question involved an overseas commodities trading company as the plaintiff and a government-owned corporation from Vietnam as the defendant. The plaintiff sought the Court's intervention to have an arbitrator appointed from the defendant's side, with the aim of establishing an arbitration tribunal to settle the issues between the parties. The defendant, however, disputed the existence of any binding arbitration agreement between the parties. 

The parties' communication was indirect, with exchanges taking place through a middleperson named Vandara Din, whom the plaintiff claimed acted on behalf of the defendant. Conversely, the defendant asserted that Vandara Din served the plaintiff. 

To proceed with arbitration under the Arbitration Act's provisions, the Court first had to determine whether an arbitration agreement was in place. Following a thorough examination of supporting documents, the Court found no evidence of an arbitration agreement between the parties and no contractual obligations arising from their email exchanges. 

Furthermore, even in the absence of a pre-existing arbitration agreement, the Court held that the parties were still free to agree to arbitrate. As a result, the Court's order was vacated, effectively dismissing the plaintiff's request.
statutes_cited:
Agrocorp 
                                Int. Pte Ltd Vs. Vietnam Northern Food Corp. (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 213 Key Words: Arbitration agreement; Mutual Consent; consensus 
                                  ad idem; Sections 9, 12, 17 of the Arbitration 
                                  Act, 2001
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 8] manual

facts_summary: The validity of a Power of Attorney executed to file a writ petition was put to the test in this case.  The petitioner contended that since the power of attorney was a general one and not an irrevocable one, the provisions of Rule 10(5) of the Power of Attorney Rules 2015 did not apply in their situation, rendering unnecessary the endorsement by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and payment of stamp duties.  In contrast, the High Court Division scrutinized various sections of the Power of Attorney Act 2012 and the Power of Attorney Rules 2015 and determined that all types of powers of attorney, whether special, general, or irrevocable, require adherence to the procedure set forth in Rule 10(5) K, L, M when executed outside Bangladesh.  The Petitioners having failed to comply with this requirement, the Rule was discharged as the writ petition was deemed to be improperly filed and thus not maintainable in its current form.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 11] manual

facts_summary: The case involved a claim for specific performance of a contract by the plaintiff, despite a substantial unperformed obligation remaining. Initially, the plaintiff failed to pay the full balance of the consideration at the time the lawsuit was commenced, only depositing a lesser amount a short time later. The defendant, in response, sought to have the complaint dismissed under Order VII Rule 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure 1908. However, the trial court denied the defendant's motion to dismiss the complaint. In a subsequent appeal, the High Court Division reviewed sections 15 and 21A of the Specific Relief Act and determined that the complaint should have been dismissed due to the plaintiff's failure to deposit the full balance of the consideration at the time the suit was filed. The High Court Division also issued a directive to lower courts to adhere strictly to statutory provisions and established case law as pronounced by the nation's highest court.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] HCD 13] declared

facts_summary: In a recent case involving Meejab Limited, an exporter of garments, the company initiated Suit No. 127 of 2021 to prevent payments under several letters of credit (LCs) opened in favor of its suppliers. The plaintiff alleged that the suppliers, defendants 1-5, conspired with defendants 6 and 7 to deceive Meejab Limited by presenting fake delivery challans in exchange for payments, despite not providing the contracted fabrics. Following the rejection of the plaintiff's application for a temporary injunction by the trial court on February 9, 2021, Meejab Limited appealed the decision. The High Court initially intervened, issuing an interim injunction in March 2021 that prohibited payments for an eight-week period, which was subsequently extended multiple times. The suppliers argued that the goods were supplied in accordance with the LCs, supported by invoices, delivery challans, and shipping documents, and that Meejab Limited had utilized the materials to manufacture and export finished garments abroad. The suppliers also contended that a bank's obligation to honor an LC is absolute and independent of any disputes between the buyer and seller, unless clear evidence of fraud is presented. Meejab Limited later attempted to introduce additional evidence suggesting that the fabrics were replaced with yarn, but the court found these claims to be inconsistent and made at a late stage. A larger bench of the High Court, following a reference from the Appellate Division, held that no evidence of fraud had been established and reaffirmed the established principle that payments under LCs cannot be restrained. The court observed that Meejab Limited failed to raise objections at the time of delivery and had already benefited from the goods, making its allegations of fraud an afterthought. Consequently, the High Court dismissed the appeal and discharged the connected civil rule, thereby vacating the earlier injunction and allowing the bank to proceed with payments under the disputed LCs.
statutes_cited:
Meejab 
                                  Ltd Vs. AA 
                                  Knitspin Ltd (Md. Mozibur Rahman Miah, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Letter of Credit, Injunction, Delivery of Challan
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 6] rejected

facts_summary: Lahmeyer International Pally Power Services Limited initiated a Money Execution Case No. 05 of 2009, pursuing enforcement of a foreign arbitral award from the Singapore International Arbitration Centre, which was rendered on October 2, 2006. A year and a half after the case was filed on September 30, 2009, before the District Judge, Dhaka, the decree-holder persisted in their efforts for nearly eight years. However, on May 4, 2017, the decree-holder failed to appear, leading to a default dismissal of the execution case. In response, the decree-holder submitted a petition for restoration of the case on May 11, 2017, which the judgment-debtors, Rural Power Company Limited, opposed vehemently. Ultimately, the District Judge reinstated the case on November 29, 2017, following payment of a cost of Tk. 5,000. Rural Power Company Limited then appealed this decision before the High Court Division, invoking Section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure. In its appeal, the judgment-debtor contended that, once an execution case is dismissed due to non-appearance, the proceedings cannot be reinstated, and the decree-holder must initiate a new case. Conversely, Lahmeyer International Pally Power Services Limited argued that their absence on May 4, 2017, was due to a clerical error in the cause list, and that the court's discretionary power under Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure should be exercised in exceptional circumstances, particularly when the dismissal was partly the result of a court's mistake. The High Court Division ruled that, although the Code of Civil Procedure does not explicitly provide for the reinstatement of a dismissed case, the court may, pursuant to Section 151 of the Code of Civil Procedure, exercise its inherent powers in extraordinary circumstances, such as when the dismissal resulted from a court's error. Consequently, the Rule was discharged, the restoration order was upheld, and the executing court was directed to expedite the disposition of the case.
statutes_cited:
Rural 
                                  Power Company Ltd Vs. Lahmeyer 
                                  International Pally Power Services Ltd (Mahmudul Hoque, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Execution, Restored the Execution Case, Order 
                                  21- Rule 11 of CPC, Section 48 of CPC, Article 
                                  182 of Limitation Act, Inherent Power of the 
                                  Court.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 7] directed to

facts_summary: A registered contract for sale, entered into by minors at the time, was brought before the courts after the defendant, having accepted nearly all consideration, refused to finalize the transaction. The minors then sought specific performance through a lawsuit, which was initially dismissed by the trial court and later upheld by the appellate court, citing section 11 of the Contract Act, 1872. According to this provision, minors are not legally capable of entering into binding contracts. The minors appealed to the High Court Division, invoking its revisional authority under section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure. The High Court found that while contracts entered into by minors cannot be enforced against them, minors still have the right to enforce contracts that were made to their benefit. However, the Court ruled that the instant suit was prohibited by section 21A(b) of the Specific Relief Act, 1877, as the minors failed to deposit the remaining consideration at the time of filing the lawsuit, a statutory requirement. Consequently, despite its disagreement with the earlier courts' reasoning, the High Court found no merit in the minors' appeal and discharged the Rule.
statutes_cited:
Mst 
                                  Nurunnahar Iqbal and others Vs. Md. 
                                  Rustom Ali Bepari and others (Md Atoar Rahman, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key words: Validity of Minor’s contract; Section 11 of Contract Act, 1872; Section 21A, Specific Relief Act, 1877; Depositing 
                                  balance of consideration pending suit for specific 
                                  performance;
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 13] affirmed

facts_summary: The writ petitioner's appointment as a Junior Officer was explicitly stated in her appointment letter to be a temporary position without specifying the duration of her engagement. The authority assigned her various tasks during her tenure, which implied her satisfactory performance, and consequently, she received a salary increase. However, the authority subsequently issued a notice requiring her response within a week as to why she should not be terminated due to alleged unsatisfactory performance. The writ petitioner responded by emphasizing her satisfactory performance but her reply was disregarded, and without affording her a personal hearing, she was removed from service. The High Court Division subsequently ordered the writ respondent to reinstate the writ petitioner. Following an appeal, the Appellate Division found that the writ petitioner could not be considered a temporary appointee because her appointment letter lacked any specific time period for her engagement. Furthermore, the Court ruled that the principle of natural justice necessitates providing an opportunity for the writ-petitioner to be heard prior to affixing a stigma of incompetence. The Appellate Division held that merely referencing incompetence in the removal order constituted arbitrary action on the part of the authority. Consequently, the appeal was dismissed.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 7] manual

facts_summary: A dispute arose between Hakkani Publishers and the Ministry of Information. The publishers alleged that they were contractually bound to supply 2317 sets of the Dalilpatra, a 15-volume publication, to the Ministry following the issuance of a work order. Nevertheless, the Ministry failed to settle the account. It was subsequently discovered that no formal work order had been issued. The Appellate Division examined the exchange of correspondence between various government departments regarding the purchase of 2317 Dalilpatra sets, which took place without proper tendering procedures. Concluding that these internal communications lacked the necessary authority and formalities, the Court ruled that they did not constitute a binding agreement between Hakkani Publishers and the Ministry of Information. As a result, the publishers were not obligated to purchase any books from the Ministry. Furthermore, the Court determined that legitimate expectations could not be based on departmental notes, as they were deemed internal communications and lacking in official status.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                & ors Vs. Golam Mustafa (Jahangir Hossain, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 155 Key Words: Legitimate expectation; work order; Article 
                                  102 of the Constitution; Dalilpatra
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] AD 8] manual

facts_summary: The central issues in this case involved determining (i) the exact timeframe within which an appeal under Section 100(2) of the Trade Mark Act, 2009, can be submitted in response to an order or decision made by the Registrar of the Department of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks, and (ii) whether the provisions of Section 5 of the Limitation Act, 1908, can be invoked to excuse a delay in filing an appeal under the relevant section of the Act. Through a comprehensive examination of the Trade Mark Act, 2009, and the Trade Mark Rules, 2025, the High Court Division ultimately resolved that the allotted time period for appealing under Section 100(2) is two months, with the clock commencing from the receipt of the certified copy of the Registrar's order or decision. Furthermore, the Court ruled that the Trade Mark Act, 2009, being a special statute, precludes the application of Section 5 of the Limitation Act, 1908, to justify a delay in submitting an appeal under Section 100(2) of the Trade Mark Act, 2009.
statutes_cited:
Kazi 
                                Md. Kamrul Islam Vs. Registrar, Dep. of PDTM & ors (Farah Mahbub, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 1 Key Words: Section 5, 29(2) and Article 156 of the 1st 
                                  Schedule of the Limitation Act, 1908; Section 
                                  2(12), 100 of the Trade Mark Act, 2009; Supreme 
                                  Court of Bangladesh (High Court Division) Rules, 
                                  1973; Article 107(1) of the Constitution of 
                                  the People’s Republic of Bangladesh; Order XLI 
                                  Rule 1, Order XLIII Rule 2 of the Code of Civil 
                                  Procedure; Rule 10, 14, 15 and 50(1) of Trade 
                                  Mark Rules, 2015;
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 1] manual

facts_summary:
A petition was filed by the petitioner, contesting a decision made by the District Judge of Dhaka in an Arbitration Miscellaneous Case. The court had denied the petitioner's request to procure the records of arbitration proceedings from the arbitrators. The central issue was whether a civil revision could be lodged against an interim court order issued under Section 42 of the Arbitration Act, 2001. To settle this matter, the Chief Justice established a Special Bench under the Supreme Court of Bangladesh (High Court Division) Rules 1973, Rule 1C, to provide a final determination.

In its deliberation, the Court examined various provisions of the Arbitration Act, 2001, the General Clauses Act, 1897, and relevant case law. Ultimately, the Court concluded that the term 'Court of District Judge' as mentioned in the Arbitration Act, 2001, was the intended meaning, and not 'persona designata'. Consequently, any decision made under this Act would be subject to a civil revision under the Code of Civil Procedure, thereby enabling the civil revision to be lodged. However, the Court dismissed the civil revision, asserting that since the petitioner had appointed an arbitrator, he could have easily obtained a copy of the proceeding from the appointed arbitrator.

The Court further observed that it was the Government's responsibility to formulate necessary regulations detailing how long and under what conditions the arbitrators would preserve the records of arbitration proceedings following the issuance of the arbitral award.
