statutes_cited:
Mitul 
                                Properties Ltd Vs. M.N.H. Bulu (Khizir Ahmed Choudhury, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 257 Key Words: Persona Designata; Maintainability of Civil 
                                  Revision; Arbitral Award; Supervisory Jurisdiction 
                                  of the High Court Division; The Arbitration 
                                  Act, 2001
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 12] no merit

facts_summary: Danish Food Limited brought forth an action under the 2009 Trade Marks Act, specifically Sections 42 and 51, to have a trade mark registration for the word 'RANI' revoked. The registration had been granted by the respondent, on behalf of the respondent's client. The High Court Division weighed the arguments and concluded that Section 51 was inapplicable to this situation. Additionally, the court determined that the requirements outlined in Section 42 of the Act had not been satisfied, thus justifying the denial of the petition. As a result, the application for removal was ultimately rejected.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] HCD 9] rejected

facts_summary:
In a litigation concerning a land transaction, the petitioning party demanded that the terms of a 1978 agreement for land purchase be carried out as intended. 
The complainant had earlier remitted Tk. 18,000 as a deposit to secure his interest in the property but the respondent refused to complete the sale by executing a deed of conveyance. 
The respondents, however, asserted their ownership rights to the land through an independent title derived from a registered gift deed recorded in 1972 and further alleged the existence of a fictitious agreement.
statutes_cited: Specific Relief Act Section 25(a); Code of Civil Procedure
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 4] Case remanded for expert comparison of thumb impressions and determination of third-party title and possession; plaintiff gets proper adjudication

appellant: Md. Badaruddin being dead his heirs
facts_summary: In a case revolving around the enforcement of a Bainapatra contract, the plaintiff endeavored to secure compliance with the terms governing the sale of real estate through the granting of specific performance. The defendant, however, contended that the passage of time within the framework of arbitration proceedings should suspend the running of the applicable limitation period, effectively extending the timeframe within which the plaintiff could initiate action. The court ultimately ruled that principles of equity favor diligence and that the prerequisite for securing specific performance, namely the timely filing of a lawsuit, was not satisfied.
statutes_cited: Limitation Act 1908 Article 113; Specific Relief Act
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 7] Time in arbitration did not extend limitation; specific performance denied as plaintiff did not come promptly to court

respondent: Central Procurement Technical Unit (CPTU) and others
facts_summary: A contractor disputing a rejected bid lodged an appeal with the CPTU Review Panel, whose authority to adjudicate the matter was contested. The central query revolved around the Panel's jurisdiction to either sustain or reject the appeal, in accordance with the governing statutes.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules; CPTU Review Panel jurisdiction
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 13 SCOB [2020] HCD 18] Review Panel has jurisdiction to allow appeals as well as dismiss them; dismissal without proper consideration was improper

facts_summary: A dispute arose between a tender winner and the authority after the latter refused to grant a No Objection Certificate, whereas the former had been declared successful in the procurement tender process. The petitioner subsequently appealed to the Review Panel's decision, claiming the Panel had overstepped its authority under Public Procurement Regulations Rule 60.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rule 60; CPTU jurisdiction
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 215] Review Panel exceeded jurisdiction; failure to issue NOC found manifestly arbitrary without lawful authority; petitioner entitled to NOC

appellant: Shetu International Pvt. Ltd and others
respondent: Artha Rin Adalat-2, Dhaka and another
facts_summary: A complaint was brought in an appeal against a decision made by the Artha Rin court, alleging that the bank had breached a fundamental requirement by publishing a summons in the newspaper without first seeking court authorization. Moreover, it was claimed that the bank failed to comply with statutory protocol under Section 7(1) of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003, as they neither employed the services of a process server nor utilized postal delivery to effect service prior to the newspaper publication.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 6(2) and 7(1)
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 9 SCOB [2017] HCD 157] Newspaper publication without prior court order found contrary to mandatory procedural requirements; bank cannot take unilateral steps

facts_summary: A litigant entered into a bid for a parcel of land as part of a public procurement process, made a deposit as stipulated by the governing body upon accepting the bid, yet the authority attempted to alter the valuation and the terms of the agreement subsequent to the bid's acceptance. The court looked at the underlying rules governing offers and acceptances in the context of contractual obligations.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872; principles of offer and acceptance; legitimate expectation
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 59] Government bound by tender offer once accepted with payment; sale binding from moment tender conditions complied with

facts_summary: In a landmark case involving a loan from a financial institution, the court was tasked with determining the scope of authority granted to Artha Rin courts under the Artha Rin Adalat Ain statute. The plaintiff, a borrower, had invoked the court's jurisdiction to absolve him from incurring excessive interest charges on the loan in question.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 50
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 7 SCOB [2016] HCD 156] Court has no power to exempt defendant from interest liability; only the financial institution itself can waive interest on its loans

appellant: Jafri Soap and Chemical Industries and others
facts_summary: The applicant invoked an alternative remedy under the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003, disputing the determinations made in the Artha Rin proceedings. In doing so, the court considered whether the disputing parties could circumvent the compulsory stipulations outlined in the aforementioned legislation.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 19, 20, 41, 42
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 79] No court can entertain application ignoring mandatory Artha Rin Adalat Ain provisions; parties cannot bypass statutory remedies

facts_summary: A global trading firm initiated proceedings to have an arbitrator selected by a Vietnamese government-controlled entity to comprise an arbitration panel. The Vietnamese entity contested the existence of a valid arbitration agreement. The communication channel between the two parties involved a broker whose standing and authority were subject to dispute. The court scrutinized whether emails exchanged between the parties gave rise to enforceable contractual commitments.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Sections 9, 12, 17
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 213] Rule discharged; no arbitration agreement found between parties; email communications did not create contractual obligation

facts_summary: In a case of great import, a party sought to contest an intermediate judicial decision rendered in a matter before an arbitration tribunal. The pertinent issue revolved around whether a civil appeal could be lodged against this decision, in accordance with the provisions of the Arbitration Act of 2001, which governed the proceedings.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 42; Code of Civil Procedure Section 115
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 257] Rule discharged; civil revision maintainable but the application was unnecessary as petitioner could obtain records from his own arbitrator

respondent: Judge, Artha Rin Adalat, Chattogram and others
facts_summary: An action was commenced by the guarantor, the petitioner, who challenged a court order imposing civil detention in a case involving a debt repayment dispute, known as an Artha Rin execution case. The petitioner claimed the bank failed to comply with the statutory requirements under Section 34, necessitating the issuance of the detention order. In its decision, the court discerned a distinction between individuals subject to criminal proceedings, known as accused persons, and those who have been found liable for a debt, referred to as judgment debtors. The court ruled that, unlike criminal accused, judgment debtors are not obligated to surrender to the court.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 2, 4, 5, 6(1), 6(5), 34, 41, 44
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 20] Rule made absolute; civil detention order quashed as bank did not file application under Section 34 with proper verification or affidavit

facts_summary: A litigant sought judicial review of a judicial decision approving a six-and-a-half-month delay in submitting a request to set aside an arbitration decision under the Arbitration Act of 2001, specifically under Section 42 of the Act. The pivotal issue revolved around the applicability of Section 5 of the Limitation Act to justify the delay in arbitration award challenge proceedings.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 42; Limitation Act 1908 Sections 5 and 29
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 57] Rule made absolute; Section 5 of Limitation Act not applicable to special law with specific limitation period; lower court order condoning delay reversed

facts_summary: A business entity initiated a lawsuit under the Arbitration Act 2001 to prevent the transfer of assets until a dispute resolution process could take place. The opposing party disputed the existence of a binding arbitration agreement and requested a court-ordered examination by a handwriting expert to verify disputed signatures. A lower court granted permission for the handwriting expert to conduct the examination.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Sections 7A, 10, 17(ka), 19, 32; Evidence Act 1872 Section 45
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 119] Rule made absolute; lower court exceeded limited powers under Section 7K; power to examine handwriting expert vested only in arbitral tribunal under Section 17(ka)

facts_summary: A certificate holder contested the validity of certificate proceedings commenced due to an alleged loan default. The Certificate Officer prematurely initiated these proceedings without first conducting a thorough evaluation as to whether the bank's claimed debt was genuinely owed, having instead relied on a defective request letter submitted by the bank.
statutes_cited: Public Demands Recovery Act 1913 Sections 4, 6, 16; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 5(5)
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 168] Certificate proceeding quashed; Certificate Officer acted without objective satisfaction about actual outstanding dues

facts_summary: A joint venture had been designated as the successful bidder for Bangladesh Railway's online ticketing system. A rival bidder lodged an objection with the Review Panel, asserting that the evaluation process had been flawed. Despite this, the Review Panel ruled in the rival bidder's favor, recommending that the tender process be re-initiated. The initially designated joint venture contested this ruling on the grounds that the objection had been lodged beyond the allotted timeline.
statutes_cited: Public Procurement Act 2006 Sections 29, 30; Public Procurement Rules 2008 Rules 56, 57, 60
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 25] Review Panel decision set aside; respondent's complaint was barred by limitation as filed beyond the 7-day period; Review Panel failed to address limitation

facts_summary: A corporate executive and shareholder of a financially troubled business entity disputed warnings issued by Bangladesh Bank to remove him as a director of Mercantile Bank, citing his failure to repay a rescheduled loan instalment as a guarantor for the ailing company.
statutes_cited: Banking Companies Act 1991 Section 17(8); Companies Act 1994 Section 43; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 3, 5, 6
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 151] Petition dismissed; directorship vacates when director is defaulter guarantor; distinction between defaulter loanee and defaulter guarantor does not protect directorship

respondent: Court of 1st Joint District Judge and Artha Rin Adalat and another
facts_summary: Upon finalization of the Artha Rin execution proceedings, Bank secured a certificate confirming its ownership title. However, physical control over the property remained untransferred to Bank. Following the conclusion of the case, the judgment-debtor made a request to reclaim the mortgaged property by paying the amount owed. The Bank countered by asserting that, upon the completion of the execution case, the court responsible for its disposition relinquished all further authority.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 20, 33(1), 33(4), 33(7), 57; right of redemption; foreclosure
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 217] Rule discharged; court not functus officio as possession never handed over; right of redemption survives where auction not properly conducted

respondent: Advocate Md. Mozammel Haque and others
facts_summary: In a landlord-tenant dispute under the provisions of the House Rent Control Act, a lower court ordered the eviction of a monthly renter after ruling that the tenant was in default for a statement made outside the scope of the original pleadings during cross-examination. The appellate court, however, overturned this decision, declaring it to be misguided. Furthermore, the High Court determined that the eviction proceedings initiated under the Act were impermissible as they were based on the use of the premises by a third party, namely the tenant's son, which was not a sufficient ground for eviction under the statute's provisions.
statutes_cited: House Rent Control Act 1991 Section 18; Code of Civil Procedure
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 108] Trial court judgment set aside; admission must be clear and unambiguous; plaintiff failed to prove grounds for ejectment

respondent: Professor M. Shamsul Alam and others
facts_summary: In Bangladesh, Niko Resources secured gas exploration concessions via a tainted transaction, where illicit remuneration was exchanged to gain approval for the Joint Venture Agreement and the Gas Purchase and Sale Agreement. The High Court Division ruled both agreements lacked the necessary legal basis, thereby stripping them of validity. This determination was subsequently upheld by the Appellate Division, which also validated the attachment of the company's assets.
statutes_cited: Contract Act 1872 Section 23; Penal Code Sections 161, 162, 163; UNCAC Articles 31, 51, 53, 54
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] AD 6] Petition dismissed; contracts procured by corruption void ab initio; no one can benefit from their own wrong; JVA and gas sale agreement declared without lawful authority

facts_summary: The decree holder bank, in the course of an execution suit, received Tk. 62,50,000 from the judgment debtors while the main lawsuit was still pending. Notably, the bank failed to apply these payments towards the subsequent execution proceedings. Consequently, the executing court intervened, making an adjustment for the amount paid, which however strayed beyond the scope of the original decree. The bank contested the executing court's ruling, contending that it overstepped its powers.
statutes_cited: Code of Civil Procedure; execution of money decrees; limits of executing court jurisdiction
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 11 SCOB [2019] HCD 28] Executing court's order set aside as it travelled beyond the decree; executing court can adjust amounts paid during execution pendency but cannot go beyond the decree itself

facts_summary: A lawsuit was filed by the plaintiff seeking enforcement of a sales agreement through a dated affidavit bearing the 3rd of April, 1995. Despite the Registration Act's Section 17B taking effect on July 1, 2005, the plaintiff failed to register the agreement within the six-month statutory deadline from that date. Furthermore, the plaintiff did not bring the lawsuit for specific performance within the subsequent six-month period following the expiration of the registration deadline.
statutes_cited: Registration Act 1908 Section 17B; Code of Civil Procedure Order 7 Rule 11
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 9 SCOB [2017] HCD 127] Plaint rejected; contract for sale became void after failure to register or file suit within prescribed periods under Section 17B of Registration Act

facts_summary: A writ petition was filed against a court order issued by the Artha Rin Adalat, notwithstanding the availability of an alternative statutory appeal route. The court ruled that the Artha Rin Adalat Ain constitutes a distinct legislative framework with a paramount provision, which necessitates adherence to its bespoke procedural guidelines. Consequently, an aggrieved party is bound to pursue the prescribed appeal process, rather than invoking writ jurisdiction.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 6, 19, 41
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 9 SCOB [2017] HCD 140] Writ petition not maintainable against Artha Rin decree; special law prescribes special procedure and appellate forum which must be exhausted first

facts_summary: A power company endeavored to invoke the authority of a writ jurisdiction to dispute a purchase agreement it had with the Bangladesh Power Development Board (BPDB) for buying electricity under a leasing arrangement. In reviewing this matter, the court investigated whether the contract in question was a statutory obligation or a typical commercial agreement. It was determined that the BPDB did not enact the contract through the exercise of statutory authority and that it was instead a purely commercial transaction.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102; Arbitration Act 2001; commercial contract principles
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 1] Writ petition not maintainable; purely commercial contract between private party and BPDB cannot be challenged through writ jurisdiction; petitioner must seek remedy through contractual/civil forums

facts_summary: A litigant disputed the validity of a second execution proceeding initiated against him, given that the initial execution proceedings had lapsed beyond the 6-year timeframe stipulated under Section 28(4) of the Ain. The court analyzed whether the second execution proceeding was time-barred, taking into account that the prior case had neither reached a conclusion nor been disposed of within the mandated 6-year period.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 28 and 33
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 15] Second execution case barred by limitation; Section 28(4) bars new execution case filed after expiry of 6 years from the date of filing of the first execution case

facts_summary: A promise made by the Sylhet City Corporation had created a reasonable expectation among the members of the petitioner-samity that a proposed market would be constructed following the removal of sheds from the Bus Terminal. However, the Corporation neglected to fulfill this obligation.
statutes_cited: Administrative law; legitimate expectation doctrine; municipal authority obligations
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 23] Legitimate expectation should be fulfilled; City Corporation Authority made a clear commitment to petitioner-samity and is bound by it

facts_summary: The parties involved in the case had previously signed agreements with BPDB, which included stipulations for resolving disputes through arbitration in accordance with the Arbitration Act 2001. In a misguided attempt, the petitioner sought to utilize the writ jurisdiction to interpret and dispute the validity of these agreements, rather than adhering to the arbitration mechanism that was mutually agreed upon.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 7; Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 84] Writ not maintainable; Section 7 of Arbitration Act restricts judicial intervention in matters covered by arbitration agreement; petitioner who signed contract with open eyes must use arbitration

facts_summary: A litigant was simultaneously subject to prosecution under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act and to enforcement proceedings in the Artha Rin Adalat, both stemming from the same loan agreement. As a result, the litigant would be compelled to post a combined 50% security in both proceedings, thereby approximating the entirety of the debt claimed by the creditor, potentially resulting in unfair enrichment in favor of the creditor.
statutes_cited: Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 Section 138; Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 41; Code of Criminal Procedure Section 344
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 8 SCOB [2016] HCD 100] Criminal proceedings stayed pending Artha Rin suit; double proceedings over same loan transaction constitutes absurd inconvenience; ends of justice demand single forum for same transaction

respondent: Artha Rin Adalat, Dhaka and others
facts_summary: The plaintiff was at the top of the bidding list in an Artha Rin auction but did not pay the remaining 75% of the bid within the allotted timeframe. Rather than making the payment directly, the plaintiff filed a request to be allowed to deposit the amount, which led to the relinquishment of the auction purchaser's statutory entitlements by their actions.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 33, 38, 45; right of redemption
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 19] Petitioner lost statutory privilege by abandonment; by filing application instead of depositing directly, auction purchaser waived all rights to the property and forfeited the earnest money

respondent: Judge, Artha Rin Adalat and others
facts_summary: In a dispute over a loan, the bank had incorrectly included an individual as a defendant in an Artha Rin suit. This party, however, did not have any legal liability as a borrower, guarantor, or mortgagee in relation to the loan debt. The court looked to Section 6(5) of the Ain, which outlines the circumstances under which someone must be joined as a necessary party in an Artha Rin case.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 6 and 6(5)
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 102] Suit dismissed against petitioner; person who is not borrower, guarantor or mortgagor cannot be made a necessary party in Artha Rin suit under Section 6(5)

facts_summary: A complaint was submitted to the Artha Rin Adalat court without the necessary supporting affidavit, as specified under Section 6 of the relevant law. The affidavit serves as an attestation to the veracity of the information presented in the complaint and the accompanying documents. Consequently, the complaint's validity was nullified due to non-compliance with the statutory requirement.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Section 6
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 6] Impugned order set aside; plaint without mandatory affidavit is invalid; non-compliance with the mandatory affidavit requirement cannot be sustained in law

appellant: Shoel Textile Mills Ltd and others
facts_summary: A financial agreement was restructured by a lending institution on behalf of the claimant. Following the restructuring, efforts were made by the institution or an associated entity to classify the claimant as being in default of loan obligations. In evaluating this matter, the court's attention was drawn to Section 5 GaGa of the Bank Companies Act 1991, which provides a legislative definition of a loan defaulter.
statutes_cited: Bank Companies Act 1991 Sections 5 GaGa and 27 KaKa
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 74] Person whose loan has been rescheduled cannot be treated as loan defaulter; rescheduling is the bank's admission that the borrower is not a defaulter under the statutory definition

facts_summary: In a dispute revolving around a land transaction, the claimant had initially concluded a contractual agreement for the purchase of land situated within the Chittagong Hill Tracts. The respondent accepted payment from the claimant, signed the agreement, and handed over control of the land. The defendant parties acquiesced to transferring ownership of the land through a registered deed, mediated by the court. It is noteworthy that the Chittagong Hill Tracts' regulations mandate prior approval for land transfers to be secured only by non-residents of the same hill district.
statutes_cited: Rangamati Hill District Local Government Council Act 1989 Section 64; CHT land transfer rules
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 101] Specific performance of bainapatra decreed; CHT law does not bar transfer to inhabitant of same hill district; prior approval only needed for non-inhabitants

facts_summary: A former Additional District Judge, having served for nineteen years in a judicial capacity, brought a Writ Petition to contest the constitutionality of Rule 300 of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I. The petitioner had submitted his resignation from service and was subsequently approved for pension and other benefits by the Ministry of Law, Justice and Parliamentary Affairs. A memo issued by the Office of the Comptroller and Auditor General, Bangladesh, however, asserted that the petitioner was not entitled to receive pension due to the forfeiture of his service, as stipulated by Rule 300(a) of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I. In response, the petitioner invoked the High Court Division's Writ jurisdiction. The High Court Division determined that the portion of Rule 300(a) pertaining to the 'forfeiture of pension in the event of resignation from service' exceeded the bounds of the Constitution, as the principle of treating an employee with an unblemished service record in the same manner as a guilty employee who was dismissed from service is inherently flawed. The court recognized that there is a significant distinction between resignation and dismissal. Nevertheless, the court deemed the remaining provisions of Rule 300 (a) and Rule 300 (b) to be valid.
statutes_cited:
Md 
                                Mahboob Murshed Vs. Bangladesh & ors (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 7 Key Words: Article 102 of the Constitution of the People’s 
                                  Republic of Bangladesh; ultra vires; Rule 300 
                                  of the Bangladesh Service Rules, Part I; the 
                                  due process; resignation; forfeiture; doctrine 
                                  of severability
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 2] declared

facts_summary:
This appeal, brought by the paternal grandparents of two minors, was initiated following the untimely passing of their son, the children's father. Their petition, filed through a writ, sought to have the minors produced before the High Court to verify that they were not being unlawfully detained in the custody of the children's mother. The maternal grandmother countered the petition, revealing that a concurrent lawsuit regarding the minors' custody was pending in the Family Court. The court had issued various directives granting the paternal grandparents visitation rights to the minors, yet the maternal grandmother had allegedly denied them these access permissions. Consequently, the grandparents had been compelled to seek relief through the writ petition. 

Upon direct interaction with the minors, the High Court Division discerned that the children exhibited a strong bond with their mother and a notably strained relationship with the paternal grandparents. In determining the best interests of the minors in such cases, the High Court emphasized that the welfare of the minor should be the paramount consideration. The High Court subsequently decided that the minors' well-being would be best served in the custody of their mother, pending the resolution of the concurrent lawsuit in the Family Court. However, it was ruled that the paternal grandparents could visit the mother's residence at her discretion and that they could meet the minors at any agreed-upon location, date, and time, though these terms would not impose any obligation on the mother. Furthermore, the court directed the Family Court to expedite the trial proceedings in the pending family suit.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 9] manual

facts_summary: Bangladesh Railway released an invitation for proposals to develop, supply, install, commission, maintain, and transfer technology for an online Integrated Ticketing System. A joint venture named SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN participated and was declared technically suitable along with four other bidders, including the respondent No. 09. After reviewing financial proposals, the Tender Evaluation Committee determined SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN as the lowest responsive tenderer. Following this, a notification was issued announcing the award. Notwithstanding this, respondent No. 09 filed a complaint under the Public Procurement Rules 2008, alleging the Tender Evaluation Committee's actions were unlawful. Later, respondent No. 09 lodged an appeal with Review Panel-2, asserting the evaluation process was flawed. SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN and other concerned parties contested the complaint. Despite this, Review Panel-2 ultimately allowed the appeal and recommended re-tendering. SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN then challenged this decision in the High Court Division. The High Court Division ruled that respondent No. 09's complaint was filed outside the time frame set by law, thereby rendering it time-barred. Moreover, the Review Panel-2's decision was found to be unenforceable due to its failure to address the issue of the statute of limitations.
statutes_cited:
SHOHOZ-SYNESIS-VINCEN 
                                JV Vs. CPTU & ors (Farah Mahbub, J) 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 25 Key Words: Article 102 of the Constitution of the People’s 
                                  Republic of Bangladesh ; Rules 8, 36(3) 56, 
                                  57, 60, 98, 102 of the Public Procurement Rules, 
                                  2008; Sections 29, 30 of the Public Procurement 
                                  Act, 2006
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 3] declared

facts_summary: A buyer, who had participated in a court-ordered auction for a seized ship, petitioned the High Court Division to release the vessel from the Marshal's custody without requiring the payment of customs duties and Value-Added Tax (VAT). The buyer claimed he was entitled to this relief based on prior correspondence from a Customs official indicating that no customs duties would be levied on the vessel. Furthermore, the buyer alleged that, as the ship was sold as scrap, a Bill of Entry was necessary for assessing customs duties, and since this requirement was not fulfilled, the demand for customs duties was inapplicable. In response, a High Court Division review of sections 18, 23, 43, 44, 45, 51, 52, 53, and 79 of the Customs Act, as well as relevant provisions within the Import and Export Act, concluded that foreign ships brought into Bangladesh, whether with or without Bills of Entry, are subject to customs duties. The court subsequently dismissed the buyer's request, ordering that the vessel be released upon payment of customs duties and other owed government levies.

Chattogram

                                  of the Customs Act, 1969; Section 2(c), 3(1) 
                                  and 3(2) of the Imports and Exports (Control) 
                                  Act, 1950

facts_summary: A proceeding was initiated by the claimant who contested his forced retirement from Biman Bangladesh Airlines Ltd as being unjust, with the CEO and Managing Director of the airline asserting their authority to render the decision. Nevertheless, the CEO's actions were contradicted by the claimant, who argued that the authority vested in the CEO was unfounded. The respondent relied on Section 114 of the Evidence Act, specifically illustration (e), which presumes that official actions are executed diligently and in conformity with the law, thereby refuting the claimant's assertions of malfeasance. Ultimately, the High Court Division evaluated the pertinent legislation and the evidence presented, finding that the CEO's decision to retire the claimant was invalid as it was not supported by a resolution from the board of directors of Biman Bangladesh Airlines Ltd and the required delegated authority, thus rendering the CEO's actions unlawful, capricious, and motivated by an ulterior intention.

facts_summary: A case before the third Labor Court in Dhaka, filed under the Bangladesh Labor Act, 2006, involved alleged labor law infractions by the GTC, which included: failing to convert temporary employees to permanent positions after the completion of their probationary periods; denying annual leave with pay or other benefits; and neglecting to establish and fund labor participation and welfare funds, as required by the Sramik Kollan Foundation Act, 2006. In response, the GTC claimed that the lawsuit was time-barred due to non-compliance with the six-month limitation period set forth in Section 314 of the Labor Act, 2006, and that no evidence linked the petitioner to the alleged infractions; the petitioner further argued that, as a Nobel laureate and internationally renowned figure, they were not involved in the GTC's financial or administrative affairs, and that the organization's nonprofit status, registered under Section 28 of the Companies Act, 1991, exempted it from contributing to labor participation funds. They also argued that, as the GTC operates in the telecommunications sector through contracts with other companies, its employees are employed on a contractual basis, rendering the Labor Court's jurisdiction an abuse of process. The High Court Division, after examining relevant laws and rules and considering the parties' admissions, determined that the petitioner's contentions were untenable due to the fact that the issue of limitation is a mixed question of law and facts that cannot be resolved without evidence being presented; Section 28 of the Companies Act, 1991, does not shield any company from contributing to labor welfare funds; and the Memorandum and Articles of Association of the GTC indicate that its Board of Directors exercises full control over the organization and is accountable for its management and administration. Therefore, the court ruled that it could not be conclusively established at this stage that the petitioner had no role in the GTC's financial management or administration. Consequently, the Rule was discharged.
statutes_cited:
Prof. 
                                Muhammad Yunus Vs. The State (SM Kuddus Zaman, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 162 Key Words: Sections 303 (Uma), 307, 117, 314 of Bangladesh 
                                  Labor Act, 2006; Labor Welfare Foundation Law, 
                                  2006; Section 28 of the Companies Act; Bangladesh 
                                  Labor Rules, 2015
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 13] manual

facts_summary:
A dispute arose from a defaulted loan of 250,000/- taka, prompting the initiation of a certificate case against the petitioner, who was subsequently ordered to repay a monthly sum of Tk. 5,000/- on 05.02.2008. The petitioner made payments in separate installments as directed by the Certificate Officer. However, on 01.02.2016, the Certificate Officer requested information from the certificate holder regarding the outstanding balance due from the petitioner. The certificate holder responded that Tk. 5,07,766.00 remained unpaid.

Against this backdrop, the petitioner contested the legality of the certificate proceeding by applying for a Rule and stay in the High Court Division. The High Court Division deemed the petitioner's jurisdictional challenges against the certificate proceeding inadmissible, as they were governed by section 5(5) of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003. Nonetheless, the Court found the Certificate Officer's actions to be improper due to the absence of objective justification, the reliance on an inappropriately filed requisition letter, and the failure to verify whether the respondent-bank's claimed outstanding dues were actually payable at the relevant time. Consequently, the certificate proceeding was declared null and void.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                Shahin Ikbal Vs. General Certificate Officer & ors (Md. Zakir Hossain, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 168 Key Words: Section 4, 6, 16 of the Public Demands Recovery 
                                  Act, 1913; Section 5(5) of the Artha Rin Adalat 
                                  Ain, 2003; Certificate proceeding; Certificate 
                                  Officer; Writ of certiorari; Calculation of 
                                  interest
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 14] quashed

facts_summary:
A case at hand features an international commodity trading company as the plaintiff and a Vietnamese state-owned corporation as the defendant. The plaintiff sought to establish an arbitration tribunal to settle their dispute. However, the defendant contested the existence of a pre-agreed arbitration framework between the parties, arguing there was no agreement in place. 

It is worth noting that the plaintiff and defendant had not interacted directly, but rather through a third party, Mr. Vandara Din, whose role was disputed by both parties, with the plaintiff claiming he was the defendant's broker and the defendant claiming he was the plaintiff's broker. In this instance, the court recognized the necessity of first determining whether an arbitration agreement actually existed before proceeding under Section 12 of the Arbitration Act.

Upon examining the supporting documents, the court ultimately concluded that there was no arbitration agreement between the parties and also found that no contractual obligations arose from the email communications exchanged between the parties. 

Furthermore, the court ruled that even in the absence of a pre-existing arbitration agreement, the parties still retained the freedom to submit to arbitration by mutual consent. Consequently, the order was vacated.
statutes_cited:
Agrocorp 
                                Int. Pte Ltd Vs. Vietnam Northern Food Corp. (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 213 Key Words: Arbitration agreement; Mutual Consent; consensus 
                                  ad idem; Sections 9, 12, 17 of the Arbitration 
                                  Act, 2001
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 8] manual

facts_summary: A lawsuit was initiated by the petitioners, seeking relief from disciplinary actions imposed by the University authority. These measures were taken in response to allegations of hazing, which the petitioners disputed. A central issue in this case centered on whether the petitioners were granted a fair hearing prior to being penalized, as well as whether they received multiple punishments for the same infractions. Furthermore, the petitioners claimed that the university authority acted unreasonably in administering the punishments. Upon review, the High Court Division determined that the petitioners were afforded sufficient opportunity to present their defense and that the authority imposed the penalties in accordance with its Disciplinary Code. The Court also found that, given the multiple incidents of hazing that occurred on different occasions, the petitioners' assertion of being repeatedly punished for the same offense was unfounded. However, recognizing the petitioners' tender years, the Court decided to mitigate their punishment.
statutes_cited:
A.S.M. 
                                Mahadi Hassan & ors Vs. BUET & ors (J.B.M. Hassan, J) 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 33 Key Words: Ragging, Section 4, 5, 17, 21 and 24 of the 
                                  Ordinance relating to the Board of Residence 
                                  and Discipline;
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 4] manual

facts_summary: A dispute arose concerning the legitimacy of a Power of Attorney used to file a writ petition. The party executing the Power of Attorney contended that despite its execution outside Bangladesh, the document, being a non-irrevocable General Power of Attorney, falls outside the scope of Rule 10(5) of the Power of Attorney Rules 2015 and is exempt from Ministry of Foreign Affairs endorsement and stamp duties. In contrast, the High Court Division examined the provisions of the Power of Attorney Act 2012 and the Power of Attorney Rules 2015, ultimately concluding that all types of powers of attorney, whether general, special, or irrevocable, require adherence to the procedure outlined in Rule 10(5) K, L, M when executed abroad. The rule stipulates that the party holding the power of attorney must follow this procedure mandatorily. The petitioners failed to comply with this requirement, resulting in the dismissal of the writ petition as it had not been filed in the correct format.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 11] manual

facts_summary: The plaintiff initiated a lawsuit for the enforcement of a contract, seeking performance of a portion where the outstanding obligations were substantial. At the commencement of the suit, he failed to provide the remaining consideration to the court, instead, depositing it several days later, albeit in an amount less than that stipulated in the contract. In response, the defendant filed an application under Order VII Rule 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, requesting the rejection of the plaint. However, the trial court denied the application. Subsequently, the defendant lodged a revisional appeal, prompting the High Court to review the matter, specifically examining sections 15 and 21A of the Specific Relief Act. Upon evaluation, the High Court determined that the plaint should have been rejected due to the plaintiff's failure to deposit the full balance of consideration at the time of filing the lawsuit. Furthermore, the High Court ordered subordinate courts to adhere strictly to the statutory provisions and precedential case law as established by the Supreme Court.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 19 SCOB [2024] HCD 13] declared

facts_summary:
Meejab Limited, a manufacturer specializing in garments for export, initiated a lawsuit (Title Suit No. 127 of 2021) to halt payments to suppliers under several back-to-back letters of credit. The company claimed that the suppliers (respondents 1-5) colluded with others to deceive Meejab by not delivering goods (fabrics) in accordance with the contract and instead submitted fake delivery records to receive payment.

Despite the trial court's initial rejection of the application for a temporary injunction on February 9, 2021, the Higher Court initially granted a provisional order in March 2021 to temporarily suspend payments for eight weeks, which was subsequently extended multiple times.

Suppliers argued that the goods were indeed delivered in compliance with the letters of credit, supported by invoices, delivery records, and shipping documents, and pointed out that the plaintiff even used the goods to manufacture and export finished garments abroad. Additionally, suppliers contended that the bank's obligation to honor the letters of credit is unconditional and independent of disputes between the buyer and seller unless clear evidence of fraud is presented.

However, the plaintiff later attempted to introduce new evidence claiming substitution of fabrics with yarn, which the court found to be inconsistent and too late to be considered. A larger bench of the Higher Court ultimately ruled that there was no established evidence of fraud, reiterating the established principle that payments under letters of credit cannot be halted. The court noted that the plaintiff failed to raise objections during the delivery process and had already benefited from the goods, making its allegations of fraud an afterthought.

As a result, the Higher Court dismissed the appeal, discharged the connected civil case, and vacated the earlier provisional order, allowing the bank to proceed with payments under the disputed letters of credit.
statutes_cited:
Meejab 
                                  Ltd Vs. AA 
                                  Knitspin Ltd (Md. Mozibur Rahman Miah, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Letter of Credit, Injunction, Delivery of Challan
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 6] rejected

facts_summary:
In a noteworthy dispute regarding the enforcement of a foreign arbitral award, Lahmeyer International Pally Power Services Limited (LIPPS), a claimant, pursued a Money Execution Case No. 05 of 2009 in the District Judge's court at Dhaka. 

This action emanated from an arbitration award issued on October 2, 2006, and a court proceeding initiated on September 30, 2009. The claimant pursued this matter diligently for nearly eight years until May 4, 2017, when, due to unforeseen circumstances, their presence was excused by the court. Consequently, the execution case was dismissed due to default. 

Following this setback, the claimant requested the court's consideration for the restoration of the case on May 11, 2017. The judgment-debtor, Rural Power Company Limited (RPCL), opposed this appeal, claiming that the execution proceedings cannot be restored once they have been dismissed for default. According to RPCL, the claimant should have initiated a new execution case rather than seeking to revive the existing one.

However, the claimant contended that their absence on May 4, 2017, resulted from a clerical error in the court's cause list and that restoration was justified under the court's inherent powers. The High Court Division of the court was subsequently petitioned by RPCL under Section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC) to review the District Judge's restoration order.

In their argument, RPCL relied on the notion that the Code of Civil Procedure does not explicitly provide for the restoration of execution proceedings once they have been dismissed for default. Conversely, the claimant posited that, although there is no express provision for restoration, Section 151 of the CPC grants courts the authority to utilize their inherent powers in exceptional circumstances, particularly when dismissal results partially from a court's error.

By a ruling, the High Court Division held that the court's inherent powers under Section 151 of the CPC could be employed in exceptional circumstances to restore the execution proceedings, provided that the dismissal resulted partly from a court's mistake. Consequently, the court discharged the rule, upheld the restoration order, and directed the executing court to dispose of the case in a timely manner.
statutes_cited:
Rural 
                                  Power Company Ltd Vs. Lahmeyer 
                                  International Pally Power Services Ltd (Mahmudul Hoque, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Execution, Restored the Execution Case, Order 
                                  21- Rule 11 of CPC, Section 48 of CPC, Article 
                                  182 of Limitation Act, Inherent Power of the 
                                  Court.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 7] directed to

facts_summary:
In a lawsuit filed on behalf of minors who had entered into a binding contract with the respondent, a significant amount of consideration was received and accepted by the respondent, only to be subsequently refused for a sale deed. The minors then pursued a claim for specific performance in court, but the court's initial dismissal was upheld on appeal due to a determination that the contract was invalid from its inception because minors are statutorily prohibited from forming contracts under the provisions of the Indian Contract Act of 1872, specifically section 11. 

As the minors were dissatisfied with the outcome, they invoked the High Court of Division's appellate powers under section 115 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Upon review, it was established that while minors cannot be held to enforce contracts made by them, a contract executed on their behalf can, in fact, be enforced on their behalf. Nonetheless, the High Court of Division also determined that the lawsuit was inadmissible under the Specific Relief Act of 1877, section 21A(b), as the minors had failed to deposit the remaining consideration amount at the time of filing the suit as a statutory prerequisite. Consequently, despite differing from the decisions made by the trial courts below, the High Court of Division found no substance to the appeal and the Rule was therefore discharged.
statutes_cited:
Mst

                                  Rustom Ali Bepari and others (Md Atoar Rahman, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key words: Validity of Minor’s contract; Section 11 of Contract Act, 1872; Section 21A, Specific Relief Act, 1877; Depositing 
                                  balance of consideration pending suit for specific 
                                  performance;
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 13] affirmed

facts_summary: A claim was made for the enforcement of a 1978 contract to buy land, with a payment of Tk. 18,000 as earnest money having been made by the claimant. The defendant denied fulfilling their obligation to execute the sale deed. In counterargument, the defendants posited that they held an independent right to the land based on a prior registered gift deed from 1972, and further contended that the original agreement was a fabrication.
statutes_cited: Specific Relief Act Section 25(a); Code of Civil Procedure

facts_summary: A junior officer's position was initially filled on a provisional basis without a defined timeframe, as stated in the appointment correspondence. The officer was tasked with diverse responsibilities and subsequently received a salary increase, implying satisfactory job performance. However, the authority suddenly issued a disciplinary notice, requesting the officer's removal due to purported unsatisfactory performance, allowing only a week for a response. In her reply, the officer highlighted her positive work record, but her submission was disregarded, and no personal hearing was granted before her termination. The High Court ruled in favor of reinstating the officer, but this decision was later overturned by the Appellate Division. The Appellate Division held that the officer's appointment was not temporary since a specific duration was not specified in her initial letter of appointment. Moreover, it was determined that the principle of natural justice demands that an individual be given the opportunity to be heard before being labeled inefficient. The authority's decision to label the officer as inefficient without a hearing was deemed arbitrary, leading to the dismissal of the appeal.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 7] manual

respondent: Central Procurement Technical Unit (CPTU) and others
facts_summary: A contractor initiated proceedings to challenge a decision issued by a Review Panel, which had opted to exclude consideration of the merits of the contractor's procurement tender appeal. At issue was whether the Review Panel for the Centre for Procurement of the UK's Ministry of Defence (CPTU) possessed the authority to entertain an appeal or, conversely, was limited to dismissing it outright.

facts_summary: The case of Hakkani Publishers before the High Court Division involved a dispute over a purported work order from the Ministry of Information for the supply of 2317 sets of the Üid£ea¡ k¤­Ül c¢mmfœ [15 Volumes]. The appellants alleged that the Ministry failed to settle the invoice, but an investigation revealed that no such order was ever issued to the publisher. The Appellate Division subsequently examined correspondence among various government departments regarding the purchase of 2317 sets of the Dalilpatra without following proper tender procedures. In its ruling, the Appellate Division established that these inter-ministerial exchanges about purchasing additional Dalilpatra volumes without a tender process did not constitute a binding agreement between the appellants and the respondent, thus relieving the appellants of any obligation to purchase books from the respondent. Furthermore, the Court determined that reliance on internal departmental notes was not a valid basis for claims of legitimate expectation, as the letters exchanged among ministries were found to be internal in nature and not a binding contractual agreement.

facts_summary: In a case before the High Court Division, the pertinent issues at stake involved determining the applicable timeframe for pursuing an appeal under Section 100(2) of the Trade Mark Act, 2009, stemming from a decision by the Registrar of the Department of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks. Additionally, it was necessary to establish whether the provisions of Section 5 of the Limitation Act, 1908, could be utilized to excuse the delay in filing the appeal under the aforementioned section. After conducting an examination of various sections of the Trade Mark Act 2009 and the Trade Mark Rules, 2025, the High Court Division ultimately ruled that a 2-month period was permitted for appealing under Section 100(2), with the commencement of this period commencing on the date the certified copy of the Registrar's order or decision was received. The Court also concluded that the Trade Mark Act, 2009, being a specialized legislation, precluded the application of Section 5 of the Limitation Act, 1908, to justify condoning the delay in filing the appeal under Section 100(2) of the Trade Mark Act, 2009.

facts_summary: A former high-ranking government official instituted a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) under Article 102(2) of the Constitution of the People's Republic of Bangladesh, challenging the practice of extending the tenure of Officers on Special Duty (OSD) beyond the prescribed 150-day duration. This prolonged designation, without the requirement of performing any specific duty, was deemed detrimental to the welfare of taxpayers and in breach of the Constitution. As a result, a Rule was issued requesting that the government justify the ongoing practice of assigning civil servants as OSDs without any assigned duties, beyond the stipulated timeframe. Following consideration, the Rule was upheld, and the continuation of this process was deemed unconstitutional and thus, unauthorized.

facts_summary:
A high-stakes legal dispute unfolded as the Managing Director and shareholder of a borrower-company, in a capacity analogous to a representative, found themselves entangled in a precarious financial situation. The company had accumulated a substantial loan liability after failing to meet regular repayment obligations to a lender bank. To address this precarious condition, the Managing Director agreed to make a preliminary payment as part of a rescheduling arrangement but ultimately fell short of fulfilling this commitment.

The lender bank subsequently issued a letter to the borrower-company, requesting the Managing Director to settle the outstanding down payment in accordance with the requirements stipulated by Bangladesh Bank's Rescheduling Guidelines. However, the Managing Director remained in a state of inactivity, and consequently, Bangladesh Bank served a notice on the Managing Director, demanding repayment of the loan within two months, warning that failure to comply would result in automatic termination of his directorship position at the Mercantile Bank, as per Section 17 of the Banking Companies Act, 1991.

The Managing Director then filed a petition under section 17(8) of the Banking Companies Act, 1991 read with section 43 of the Companies Act, 1994, objecting to the termination of his directorship and the transfer of shares in the Mercantile Bank Ltd. The High Court Division, after conducting a thorough examination of relevant provisions of the Banking Companies Act, 1991 and the Artharin Adalat Ain, 2003, dismissed the petition, ruling that the directorship of a scheduled bank is automatically vacated if a director procures a loan for personal purposes or acts as a guarantor for another borrower. The court also highlighted the distinction between Section 17 of the Banking Companies Act and Section 5 of the Artharin Adalat Ain. Furthermore, the court imposed exemplary costs on the Managing Director for abusing the court process.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 11] manual

facts_summary: In this case, the central issue revolved around the question of whether a writ could be invoked against Milk Vita. A determination by the High Court Division established that Milk Vita should be classified as a public entity rather than a private one, thereby rendering a writ as a viable option. Additionally, the court concluded that the petitioners' status did not qualify them as "workers," thus precluding them from seeking resolution through the Labor Court. Furthermore, the court's findings showed that the impugned memoranda were issued without lawful authority, prompting the court to declare them null and void. Consequently, the court ordered the relevant authority to pursue action against the petitioners in accordance with clause 8.06 of the চাকুরি বিধি ও নিয়োগ নীতিমালা ২০০৮ (সংশোধিত ২০০৯) and to resolve the matter in compliance with the applicable laws.

                                  2006; section 2(c) of Services (Reorganisation 
                                  & Conditions) Act-1975; dismissal; termination; 
                                  termination simpliciter; Section 14, 16 of the 
                                  সমবায় সমিতি আইন ও সমবায় সমিতি বিধিমালা; Sections 
                                  14 and 21 of the Co-operative Society Act- 2001;
