facts_summary: In Civil Revision No. 1497 of 2021, Kazi Abdul Bakir challenged the Family Court at Gazipur's decision to continue a family suit initiated by his wife, Nur Taj Hossain Suchona. The petitioner initially filed a civil lawsuit seeking to declare the marriage kabinnama dated January 6, 2017, as invalid due to the opposite party's alleged suppression of a prior marriage and consequent fraudulent inducement into the present union. Subsequently, the opposite party brought a family suit claiming a dowry of Tk. 10,00,000 and maintenance. The petitioner requested a stay of the family suit pending the resolution of his civil suit, which was rejected by both the trial court and the appellate court. In revision proceedings, the petitioner's counsel argued that the two suits involved the same fundamental issue – namely, whether a valid marriage existed between the parties – and therefore, under the Code of Civil Procedure, the family suit should be stayed to prevent inconsistent findings. In contrast, the opposite party contended that the Family Courts Ordinance, 1985, granted the Family Court jurisdiction to determine the validity of marriage incidentally when such determination was essential to adjudicating claims of dowry and maintenance. After considering the relevant statutory provisions and judicial precedents, the Court ruled that, despite the earlier institution of the civil suit, the Family Court retained the authority to pronounce judgment on the validity of marriage in resolving ancillary claims. The Court emphasized the principle of avoiding multiple proceedings and the potential for effective relief in the family suit, finding no compelling reason to stay the proceedings. Consequently, the petition was dismissed, the stay order was vacated, and the Family Court was instructed to proceed with the trial at an expedited pace.
statutes_cited:
Kazi 
                                  Abdul Bakir Vs. Nur 
                                  Taj Hossain Suchona (Fatema Najib, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Keywords: Family court; Jurisdiction of family court to decide on the 
                                  validity of Kabinnama; Validity of marriage; Section 10 of CPC; Multiplicity of suits; Stay of family court proceedings pending civil 
                                  suit
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 11] directed to

facts_summary: Bangla Food and Beverage Limited contested the Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal's rejection of its appeal due to the company's failure to pay a statutory fee under Section 194 of the Customs Act, 1969, as the disputed goods remained in the custody of the customs authority. The Supreme Court determined that the statutory deposit under Section 194 only becomes applicable once the goods are no longer under customs control. Consequently, the court deemed the tribunal's decision to be null and void, lacking any legal basis or authority. The High Court subsequently ordered the Tribunal to accept and process the appeal within a timeframe of six months.
statutes_cited:
Bangla 
                                  Food and Beverage Limited Vs. The 
                                  Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal and 
                                  another (Ahmed Sohel, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Statutory Deposit; Custody of Goods; Custody 
                                  of Goods; Section 194 of the Customs Act, 1969
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 16] declared

facts_summary: A government primary school educator initiated a grievance due to an unfair dismissal from service. The educator had been serving at the school when disciplinary charges were brought against him. A one-sided investigation ensued, culminating in a second show cause notice being issued without providing the investigator's report. Consequently, the educator was unable to mount a defense. After being dismissed, the educator lodged an appeal with the Appellate authority, but the appeal was not resolved within the statutory timeframe of two months as stipulated by the Administrative Tribunal Act. Consequently, the educator approached the Administrative Tribunal in Chittagong. The Tribunal overturned the dismissal order, but the Administrative Appellate Tribunal reversed this decision on appeal. The educator then requested permission to appeal to the Appellate Division of the Supreme Court. The Appellate Division overturned the decision of the Administrative Appellate Tribunal, citing among other reasons that the educator had been denied an opportunity to cross-examine witnesses and produce evidence in his favor, in accordance with Rule 10 of the Government Servants (Discipline and Appeal) Rules, 1985.
statutes_cited:
Md. Syedul Abrar, son of late 
                                Ahmed Hossain Vs. Government of Bangladesh and others (Obaidul Hassan, J) 15 SCOB [2021] AD 102 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative 
                                  Appellate Tribunal; Departmental Inquiry Report; 
                                  Natural Justice; Disciplinary proceedings
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 15 SCOB [2021] AD 7] reversed

facts_summary: A group of individuals who were classified as work-charged employees within various governmental agencies submitted separate Writ Petitions to the High Court Division, which led to the court issuing directives to the respondents, petitioners in the writ cases, to either regularize or integrate their services within the revenue sector. In response, the government entities involved in these cases filed separate Civil Petitions for Leave to Appeal, which were subsequently dismissed due to being beyond the statute of limitations. Subsequently, the government entities filed review petitions, which were eventually disposed of by the Appellate Division. In doing so, the Appellate Division noted that service rendered by work-charged employees, spanning over two decades or more, could reasonably be regarded as equivalent to that of permanent employees, thus entitling them to pensionary benefits. Furthermore, the Appellate Division drew inspiration from various initiatives undertaken by state governments in India on behalf of work-charged employees, and accordingly recommended that the government devise a policy framework for providing pensionary and other benefits to work-charged employees who have maintained continuous service for an extended period, specifically twenty years or more.
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] AD 2] manual

facts_summary: The central issue in this case revolved around whether an employee who had taken a 6-month leave to pursue training in Japan with government approval, then extended his leave for an additional 3 months and remained absent from his post for 7 years 7 months 24 days without prior authorization, was deemed to have forfeited his status as a government employee in accordance with Bangladesh Service Rules 34 (1st Part). Contrary to this, the employee was initially permitted to rejoin his post and remained in service for approximately 1 year and 7 months. The Administrative Appellate Tribunal's decision concluded that by accepting the employee's rejoining, the Director General of Industry and Labour Wing tacitly approved the employee's unauthorized leave and waived the government's right to decline the rejoining. However, the Appellate Division reversed this decision, stating that the Director General lacked the authority to act under Rule 34 and thus his decision to permit the employee's rejoining was both unauthorized and void from the outset. Furthermore, the Court ruled that the principles of estoppels, waiver, and acquiescence cannot supersede statutory provisions and, consequently, the employee's 1 year and 7 months of service following rejoining did not constitute a waiver by the government of its clear statutory right, as stipulated in Rule 34.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                and another Vs. Sayed Mahabubul Karim ( Md. Nuruzzaman, J) 16 SCOB [2022] AD 46 Key Words: Rule 34, 1st Part of the Bangladesh Service 
                                  Rules; Doctrine of estoppels, waiver and acquiescence; 
                                  void ab intio
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] AD 7] manual

facts_summary: A former government employee submitted a petition to the court seeking resolution on a service-related issue, and the court issued a temporary order in their favour. The government then filed a petition with the Appellate Division, challenging the High Court Division's decision, asserting that even retired public servants must seek relief through the Administrative Tribunal according to section 4(3) of the Administrative Tribunal Act of 1980. The Appellate Division agreed with the government's position, acknowledging that a previous decision reported in 71 DLR (AD) 319 was incorrect in allowing a writ petition by a retired public servant. Consequently, the Appellate Division departed from its earlier decision, finding it to be an invalid precedent, and the court lifted the rule issued by the High Court Division. Notably, the Appellate Division observed that, under article 111 of the Constitution, the High Court Division is not empowered to declare any of the Appellate Division's decisions as invalid, and it is obligated to adhere to the Appellate Division's judgments in their entirety. The High Court Division can, however, bring such matters to the notice of the Chief Justice of Bangladesh. Similarly, subordinate courts lack jurisdiction to review the legitimacy of the High Court Division's judgments, as only a court of equivalent status to the one that rendered the original judgment is entitled to reconsider and potentially depart from it if it was decided incorrectly.
statutes_cited:
Secretary, 
                                Posts & Telecom Div. & anr Vs. Shudangshu Shekhar & ors (Obaidul Hassan, 
                                  J) 18 SCOB [2023] AD 11 Key Words: Per incuriam; Section 4(3) of the Administrative 
                                  Tribunal Act, 1980; Article 111 of the Constitution; 
                                  maintainability of the writ petition by a retired 
                                  public servant in service matter
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 2] manual

facts_summary: The petitioners successfully secured passing marks in the viva voce assessment and were officially notified on January 21, 2007. The Bangladesh Public Service Commission, in its capacity as the recommending authority, endorsed a list of 3,567 successful candidates, including the petitioners, for appointment to various cadres. Concurrently, certain media outlets published allegations concerning the integrity of the 27th BCS examination, which were subsequently disputed by the Bangladesh Public Service Commission. Subsequently, the Caretaker Government, exercising its extraordinary authority, made the unprecedented decision to rescind the publication of the 27th BCS examination results. In compliance with this directive, the Ministry of Establishment requested the Bangladesh Public Service Commission to re-conduct the viva voce examination of all successful candidates from the 27th BCS. As a result, a second viva voce examination was subsequently conducted and the results were published on September 23, 2008, recommending 3,239 candidates for appointment to various cadres under the 27th BCS, excluding however, 1,137 candidates, including the petitioners, who had previously cleared the first viva voce examination. A subsequent notification was issued in the Bangladesh Gazette on November 10, 2008. The petitioners, along with several other candidates who had previously secured passing marks in the first viva voce examination, filed writ petitions challenging the results published by the Bangladesh Public Service Commission on September 23, 2008, as well as an office order dated October 20, 2008. Following hearings, the High Court Division disposed of the rules through judgments and orders dated November 11, 2009, and January 26, 2010, directing the concerned respondents to appoint the candidates, including the petitioners, who had been successful in the first viva voce examination, to their respective cadres. The Government appealed against this decision, preferring Civil Petitions for Leave to Appeal Nos. 512, 513, and 514 of 2010. This Division disposed of the leave petitions by judgment and order dated July 11, 2010, setting aside the judgment and order of the High Court Division dated November 11, 2009. Aggrieved by the outcome, the petitioners preferred Civil Review Petitions Nos. 197-199 of 2024, and leave was granted by this Division by order dated November 7, 2024,
statutes_cited:
Mohammad 
                                  Sohel Rana & others Vs. Bangladesh 
                                  Public Service Commission (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Article 31,102 of the Constitution of Bangladesh; Setting aside judgment of the High Court Division 
                                  without granting leave; Principle of natural justice, audi alteram partem– 
                                  no man should be condemned unheard; Justice hurried, Justice buried;
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 3] manual

facts_summary: A petition for rectification was lodged against the Chittagong Club Ltd, a private company established under the Companies Act, 1994, by invoking Section 43 of the statute. The petition sought to restore the petitioner's name in the Members' Register through a court-ordered declaration that a General Committee decision, specifically the suspension of the petitioner's membership, was unlawful and unenforceable. After scrutinizing the Companies Act, 1994, and relevant regulations, the High Court Division ruled against the petition, finding it lacked the necessary jurisdiction and that the dispute was purely civil in nature, thus requiring resolution through the civil court. As a result of filing a groundless claim, the Company Court levied a fine of 100,000 taka against the petitioner.
statutes_cited:
Engr. Md. Anwar Hossen -VERSUS- Chittagong Club Ltd and others 15SCOB [2021] HCD 60 (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) Key Words: Golden rule of statutory interpretation; Section 
                                  43 and 44 of Companies Act, 1994; Rule 8 of 
                                  the Companies Rules, 2009; Section 2(1)(d), 
                                  Section 3(1), Section 43 and Section 233 of 
                                  the Companies Act, 1994
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 6] rejected

facts_summary: The judgment debtor, seeking to vacate an execution, filed a motion to quash the proceedings on the grounds of exceeding the statute of limitations. The presiding Senior Assistant Judge denied the request, relying on a digest of a precedent set by a Pakistani High Court in 1998, as recorded in a Digest of Laws and Reports reference book, which was subsequently upheld by the High Court Division. Nevertheless, the Appellate Division determined that the Pakistani High Court decision was inapplicable to the jurisdiction after 25th March 1971, and elaborated on which judicial precedents from the Dhaka High Court, the Federal Court of Pakistan, the Supreme Court of Pakistan, the Calcutta High Court, the Federal Court of India, and the Privy Council were binding on the court, and which were not. Ultimately, the Appellate Division concluded that the execution proceedings were launched more than three years beyond the permissible timeframe stipulated under Article 182 of the Limitation Act, and accordingly, dismissed the execution case.
statutes_cited:
Terab 
                                Ali & ors Vs. Syed Ullah & ors ( Md. Nuruzzaman, J) 17 SCOB [2023] AD 34 Key Words: Persuasive efficacy; interpretation of law; 
                                  Article 111 read with Article 149 of the Constitution 
                                  of Bangladesh, 1972
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] AD 2] affirmed

facts_summary: The Administrative Appellate Tribunal's determination that the respondent's penalty was unlawful due to being imposed by the Director General, who was holding an ongoing position but not the appointing authority, was the subject of appeal. After examining the pertinent legal provisions, the Appellate Division determined that when an individual is granted an ongoing charge without a time limitation, it is reasonable to assume that they possess full administrative and financial authority within the institution. Consequently, the Appellate Division reversed the judgment and order of the Administrative Appellate Tribunal.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                & anr Vs. Nazrul Islam Biswas (M. Enayetur Rahim, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 119 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative Appellate 
                                  Tribunal; Current Charge; Section 4 (3) (b) 
                                  of the Government Servants (Discipline and Appeal) 
                                  Rules, 1985
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 19 SCOB [2024] AD 5] manual

facts_summary: On March 21, 2005, the Senior Assistant Secretary, Ministry of Rural Development and Co-operative issued a notification designating the site for the Pashapol Union Parishad Complex Building at Pashapol. The plaintiffs, however, claimed that prior to this, the government had officially recognized a different site, namely Bariali Mouza, as the location for the Pashapol Union Parishad Office, where all necessary requirements for the construction of the complex building were readily available. The plaintiffs alleged that ignoring this site, the government's notification dated March 21, 2005, was unlawful. In response, the defendants denied the allegations and contended that Pashapol served as the central hub of the Union, featuring essential facilities such as a hut, educational institutions, a mosque, and others. The defendants sought to have the suit dismissed. Subsequently, the trial court dismissed the suit, but the appellate court reversed the decision, allowing the plaintiffs' appeal and decreeing the suit in their favor. Upon review, the High Court Division upheld the appellate court's judgment. The defendants then sought leave to appeal to the Appellate Division, which was granted. In their submission, the petitioners argued that the government's selection of the Union Parishad site constituted a policy decision, a matter of administrative discretion, and as such, the plaintiffs lacked standing to challenge this policy decision. In contrast, the respondents contended that the government had issued specific guidelines through a circular dated December 28, 2005, outlining the procedures for selecting sites, which were not adhered to in the impugned notification. The Appellate Division ultimately ruled that shifting the site of the Union Parishad involved a policy decision and administrative action, for which the plaintiffs lacked the necessary standing to challenge the government's policy-making authority, and thus, allowed the appeal and dismissed the suit.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                  Obaidul Islam and others Vs. Md.Abdul 
                                  Mazid Mondal and others, (A.K.M. Asaduzzaman, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Declaration; selection of site of Union Parishad; 
                                  representative character suit; policy decision 
                                  of government; administrative decision
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 6] appeal is allowed

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Primary and Mass Education and others
facts_summary: Primary school teachers employed through nationalization contested certain provisions in the 2013 Service Conditions Rules, which had the effect of truncating their years of service by nearly half. This change, coupled with amendments that accorded preferential seniority status to direct appointees, worked to the disadvantage of nationalized teachers in terms of promotion opportunities and career progression timelines.
statutes_cited: অধিগ্রহণকৃত বেসরকারি প্রাথমিক বিশ্ববিদ্যালয়ের শিক্ষক (চাকুরি শর্তাবলী নির্ধারণ) বিধিমালা ২০১৩ Rules 2(Ga) and 9(1)
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 8] Rule 9(1) of 2013 Rules struck down as manifestly unreasonable and self-contradictory; nationalized teachers' seniority rights protected

facts_summary: The Bangladesh Public Service Commission issued a recommendation for a candidate who had achieved the milestone of passing all four stages of the BCS examination, a prerequisite for government appointment. However, the relevant government authority chose not to offer the candidate a position without furnishing any legitimate or lawful rationale for their decision.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Civil Service Recruitment Rules 1981 Rule 4(3)(A)(B); Constitution
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 16] Refusing appointment of BPSC-recommended candidate without valid cause declared unlawful and unconstitutional

respondent: Secretary, Ministry of Civil Aviation, Bangladesh and others
facts_summary: A long-serving Biman Bangladesh Airlines staff member contested the promotion process, which relied exclusively on interview results, thereby ignoring the employee's comprehensive service records compiled over an extended period. Such a system potentially facilitated the making of arbitrary and unlawful promotion decisions.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; ACR-based evaluation principles
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 15] Promotion solely by interview found unfair; petitioner deprived unlawfully and deserves retrospective promotion

facts_summary: Teachers from government schools and colleges contested the validity of Nitimala 2012, contending that its prohibition on engaging in coaching ventures was unconstitutional. They also claimed that the Anti-Corruption Commission lacked the authority to investigate their coaching-related activities.
statutes_cited: Public Servant (Discipline and Appeal) Rules 1985; Penal Code 1860 Section 166; Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2004; Nitimala 2012
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 309] Nitimala upheld as valid supplement to existing service rules; teachers already prohibited from coaching by their service rules

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Post Telecommunications and IT and others
facts_summary: Employees filling temporary positions for an extended period of time, typically spanning a decade and a half, contested a modification to an executive order governing their potential conversion to permanent roles. Repeated extensions of their employment by the governing authority had fostered a reasonable anticipation of being absorbed into the permanent workforce.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; Constitutional rights
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 11 SCOB [2019] HCD 46] Employees' legitimate expectation of absorption upheld after years of continuous service; executive order amendment found whimsical and beyond authority

facts_summary: Temporary employees employed on a contingent basis, having served for a decade, brought a claim for permanent regularization of their positions. Despite being thoroughly satisfied with the quality of their work, the relevant authority's endorsement of regularization was left unfulfilled.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; service regularization principles
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 1] Long-serving temporary government employees cannot be denied expectation of regularization especially when their work was satisfactory

facts_summary: The petitioning doctor was denied seniority benefits due to an untimely processing of their promotion request. The applicant had initiated their promotion proceedings prior to a colleague who ultimately received the promotion, leading to an unforeseen loss of seniority for the doctor as a direct consequence of the authority's inaction.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; principles of natural justice and equity
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 34] Seniority ordered to be restored from date of colleague's promotion; writ court as court of equity cannot allow unjust loss of seniority

appellant: Md. Yousuf Ali Akon and others
facts_summary: In a service law case, petitioners were initially employed as Master Pilots on conditional terms, with the explicit assurance contained within a job posting by the Bangladesh Inland Water Transport Authority (BIWTA) that they would be permanently appointed upon successful completion of a one-year probationary period. Conversely, BIWTA failed to uphold its end of the agreement, neglecting to absorb the petitioners despite their satisfactory performance in their roles.
statutes_cited: Service regulations; legitimate expectation doctrine
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 66] Petitioners had legitimate expectation of absorption based on express promise in advertisement; BIWTA directed to permanently absorb them

facts_summary: A personnel classification change from Steno-Typist to Stenographer prompted a dispute over eligibility for upgraded time scales. The central issue revolved around whether prior benefits received as a Steno-Typist precluded entitlement to corresponding benefits in the newly held position of Stenographer.
statutes_cited: Service rules; time scale regulations; promotion benefits
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 124] Promoted Stenographer entitled to fresh time scales in new post; previous time scales as Steno-Typist do not bar entitlement in promoted post

facts_summary:
This appellate matter centered around the scope of judicial authority pertaining to the Republic for Administrative Tribunal, focusing on the tribunal's jurisdictional purview. 

At issue was the interpretation of "a person in the service of the Republic" as defined within the relevant legislation, with particular emphasis on whether the term encompasses individuals who have undergone retirement, dismissal, removal, or discharge from their positions.
statutes_cited: Administrative Tribunal Act; Constitution
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 1] Retired, dismissed and discharged government servants fall within Administrative Tribunal jurisdiction for service-related grievances

appellant: Gazi A.K.M. Fazlul Haque and others
facts_summary: A lawsuit brought by members of the Privatization Commission contested the promotional criteria and procedures in place. The court scrutinized whether a reliance solely on seniority would suffice for advancing individuals to the position of Director from that of Deputy Director, and also considered the relevance of merit and the involvement of selection committees in the promotional process.
statutes_cited: Privatization Commission Officers and Employees Service Regulations 2002; Constitution Article 102
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 4 SCOB [2015] HCD 42] Both seniority and merit required for promotion; petitioners entitled to be considered for promotion under proper criteria including minimum 5 years service

facts_summary: Petitioners objected to the disciplinary measures imposed upon them, citing a failure to adhere to the statutory requirement for issuing a show cause notice prior to such actions. They further contended that the disciplinary proceedings were not conducted by a three-member inquiry committee as mandated by the service rules, and that these proceedings were not disposed of within the sixty-day timeframe specified.
statutes_cited: Government service disciplinary rules; show cause notice requirement Rule 5.4
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 2 SCOB [2015] HCD 99] Disciplinary action set aside; mandatory show cause notice and enquiry committee requirements under service rules were not followed

respondent: Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University (BSMMU) and others
facts_summary: A constitutional challenge was lodged against a BSMMU appointment, contending that the selection committee had exceeded its authority in contravention of Article 31 of the Constitution. The central issue revolved around whether the passage of time and subsequent service in the position could be relied upon to validate appointments that were initially unlawful.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 31; BSMMU appointment rules
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 3] Court held unlawful appointments cannot be validated by service; selection committee abuse of power is ground for setting aside interview result

facts_summary: A complaint was lodged by the petitioners regarding a university Syndicate decision that impacted their entitlements. It was determined by the court that the Syndicate had acted in a manner that significantly contravened established tenets of natural justice by taking a decision that was prejudicial and did so without adhering to the principles of fairness and the rule of law.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; administrative fairness doctrine
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 13 SCOB [2019] HCD 7] Syndicate order vitiated as gross violation of natural justice; failure to act fairly leads to arbitrariness which vitiates the impugned order

appellant: Prof. Dr. Muhammad Yunus and others
facts_summary: A complaint was lodged by petitioners against a charge framing order issued under the Bangladesh Labour Act of 2006 by Grameen Telecom. An investigation by the Labour Inspector revealed non-compliance with several statutory provisions, including the requirement to convert probationary employees to permanent ones, providing employees with paid annual leave, and establishing the Labour Participation Fund and the Labour Welfare Fund.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 275] Rule discharged; charge framing order upheld; limitation question is mixed question of law and fact requiring evidence; nonprofit company still liable for labor welfare contributions

facts_summary: In a dispute involving Biman Bangladesh Airlines, an employee contested a compulsory retirement order issued unilaterally by the Chief Executive Officer/Managing Director. The employee argued that the decision was made without the requisite approval from the board of directors, despite the Articles of Association mandating a board resolution for such decisions. Moreover, the employee pointed out that no express delegation of authority existed to enable the CEO to unilaterally effect a retirement decision.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Biman Corporation Employee Regulations 1988; Bangladesh Biman Corporation Ordinance 1977; Articles of Association
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 108] Rule found merit; CEO's retirement order declared arbitrary and without jurisdiction for lack of board resolution and delegated authority

facts_summary: The Bangladesh Labour Act of 2006 was invoked by Prof. Yunus and Grameen Trust Company in a Labour Court appeal, contesting a charge framing order that they claimed had been issued in contravention of the aforementioned Act. Specifically, they alleged that their probationary employees were not being converted to permanent status, annual leave was not being granted, and a Labour Welfare Fund was not being established. As a prominent Nobel laureate, Prof. Yunus sought to dissociate himself from any financial mismanagement that might have occurred, arguing that his role in the management of the organization did not extend to financial affairs.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 162] Rule discharged; charge framing upheld; Board of Directors responsible for GTC administration; nonprofit company not exempt from Labour Welfare Fund contribution

facts_summary: A faculty member at Dhaka University disputed a University Syndicate decision downgrading her position from Associate Professor to Assistant Professor, citing plagiarism as the reason. Notably, the Syndicate's disciplinary actions against her were taken without adhering to the requisite protocols, including the failure to formally notify her of the allegations, provide a copy of the investigation report, and afford her a meaningful opportunity to mount a defense.
statutes_cited: Enquiry Committee and Tribunal Regulations 1980 Regulation 7(a); Dhaka University Order 1973 Article 52; First Statutes of Dhaka University
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 182] Syndicate decision declared illegal; mandatory charge framing and opportunity to be heard not followed; decision vitiated by bias and malafide

facts_summary: A retired Additional District Judge disputed a provision of the Bangladesh Service Rules Part I, specifically Rule 300(a), which stipulates that an individual forfeits their pension upon resignation. The aggrieved litigant had accumulated nearly two decades of unbroken service and subsequently submitted an application for pension benefits, only to see their claim rejected by the Comptroller and Auditor General.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Service Rules Part I Rule 300; Constitution of Bangladesh Articles 27, 31
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 7] Rule 300(a) on pension forfeiture upon resignation declared ultra vires the Constitution; person who resigns is entitled to pension based on length of service

respondent: Sk. Md. Abdullah Faruque and others
facts_summary: The High Court Division, in reliance on the 2018 Absorption Rules, ordered the incorporation of nationalized college instructors as government college lecturers, despite the fact that the original applicants had not specifically requested absorption under those regulations and a preliminary injunction had not been issued in this particular context. The Appellate Division, however, maintained that the High Court Division had exceeded the parameters of the preliminary injunction.
statutes_cited: Constitution Article 102; Nationalized College Teachers Absorption Rules 2018; Supreme Court HCD Rules 1973 Chapter XIA
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 6] HCD judgment set aside; court cannot grant relief beyond what was prayed for in the writ petition or the terms of the Rule Nisi

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Expatriates Welfare and others
facts_summary: The petitioner's emigration recruiting license was revoked, accompanied by the confiscation of associated securities, in a decision made unilaterally by the authority without affording the petitioner the opportunity to present its case beforehand. This action contravened the stipulations of Section 14(1) of the Emigration Ordinance 1982 which specifically requires that a hearing must take place prior to cancellation.
statutes_cited: Emigration Ordinance 1982 Section 14
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 231] Order of cancellation was bad in law; mandatory requirement of opportunity of being heard before cancellation of license was not followed

facts_summary: A group of petitioners appealed the disciplinary measures imposed by Islamic University, alleging that the institution's administration breached procedural norms. Specifically, they argued that they were not initially notified of the allegations made against them nor the proposed sanctions. Furthermore, the petitioners claimed they were denied the opportunity to provide a response prior to the university taking disciplinary action against them.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102(2); principles of due process and natural justice
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 92] Non-compliance with due process elements is fatal to authority's subsequent action; both informing of charge and opportunity to reply are mandatory before disciplinary action

respondent: Chairman, First Labour Court, Dhaka and others
facts_summary: The Labour Court's authority to hear cases involving outsourced workers was disputed by Grameenphone. As part of this dispute, the court conducted a thorough analysis of the contractual relationship between companies in Bangladesh that engage in outsourcing arrangements. Specifically, the court investigated whether the workers hired through these arrangements should be considered direct employees of the client businesses or employees of the third-party contractor companies.
statutes_cited: Labour Act 2006; outsourcing contract principles
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 7] Outsourcing is a contract of services not employment; service recipient not the employer of persons engaged by the contractor; Labour Court jurisdiction does not extend to outsourced workers

respondent: Commissioner, Customs Bond Commissionerate and others
facts_summary: A notice to show cause was issued by the Bond Commissioner against the petitioner's license, following an agreement in principle by the NBR with a predecessor's proposal. Consequently, the subsequent Bond Commissioner was bound by the terms of the NBR-approved plan and lacked the authority to independently deviate from it. Notably, the show cause notice was issued concurrently with a request to other authorities to suspend the license, thereby rendering it an inflexible and predetermined proceeding.
statutes_cited: Customs Bond regulations; natural justice principles; administrative fairness
notes: [Augmented copy 1 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 40] Closed-minded show cause notice declared unlawful; when authority has predetermined the outcome and simultaneously taken adverse action, the notice is a mere formality without legal validity

facts_summary:
An individual, formerly employed in a government position, submitted a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) application to the court under Article 102(2) of the Constitution of Bangladesh. This action challenged the government's practice of designating an employee as an Officer on Special Duty (OSD) for an extended period beyond the allowed 150 days, while the individual continued to receive salary and benefits without performing any official duties.

This situation was deemed unconstitutional and detrimental to the country's taxpayers. Consequently, a court order was issued, directing the government to justify the continued posting of civil servants as OSDs without any assigned duties. The court subsequently ruled that the government's actions were unlawful, as they exceeded the constitutional time limit of 150 days for such postings.

As a result, the court declared the practice of maintaining an officer in an OSD position beyond the stipulated period of 150 days to be unauthorized and without legal basis.
statutes_cited:
M. Asafuddowlah -VERSUS- Government of Bangladesh 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 1 (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) Key Words: Article 20(2), 31, 88 and 102 (2) of the Constitution 
                                  of the People’s Republic of Bangladesh; Constitutionality 
                                  of posting Officers on Special Duty (OSD) for 
                                  unlimited period;
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 1] declared

facts_summary: The applicant, serving as the Managing Director and shareholder of the Borrower-Company, has been embroiled in a financial dispute with a Bank, to whom the Company had borrowed a substantial sum of money. Owing to the Borrower-Company's persistent failure to make timely payments, the outstanding loan liability has ballooned significantly. In an attempt to restructure the debt, the applicant verbally agreed to make a down payment, though he did not fully comply with this commitment. Consequently, the lender Bank issued a letter to the Borrower-Company, represented by the applicant, requesting the prompt payment of the outstanding down payment in accordance with the regulations set forth by the Bangladesh Bank. Unfazed by the Bank's demands, the applicant failed to take any concrete steps to settle the debt. In response, the Bangladesh Bank subsequently served the applicant with a notice, requiring repayment of the loan within a two-month timeframe, cautioning that failure to comply would result in the automatic vacating of his position as a Director of the Mercantile Bank, pursuant to Section 17 of the Banking Companies Act, 1991. The applicant subsequently filed a petition under Section 17(8) of the Banking Companies Act, 1991, in conjunction with Section 43 of the Companies Act, 1994, to contest his directorship and shareholding in the Mercantile Bank Ltd and challenge the propriety and legality of terminating his directorship in the Bank. The High Court Division, after scrutinizing the relevant provisions of the Banking Companies Act, 1991, and the Artharin Adalat Ain, 2003, dismissed the petition, ruling that a Director of a scheduled bank automatically forfeits their position if they personally take a loan or serve as a guarantor for another borrower. Additionally, the court distinguished between the provisions of Section 17 of the Banking Companies Act and Section 5 of the Artharin Adalat Ain and imposed an exemplary cost on the applicant for abusing the judicial process.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 11] manual

facts_summary: The central issue in this matter centered around whether a writ could be filed against Milk Vita, a public body. The High Court Division ultimately concluded that the writ was indeed maintainable, ruling that Milk Vita is not a private entity but rather a public body subject to this type of judicial review. Furthermore, the court held that the petitioners do not qualify as "workers" and thus their claims do not fall within the jurisdiction of the Labor Court. The court also found that the petitioners' memos were not issued with proper authority, effectively rendering them invalid. Consequently, the court declared the memos to be unauthorized and directed the relevant authority to proceed against the petitioners in accordance with clause 8.06 of the Employment Rules and Recruitment Policy, 2008 (amended 2009).
statutes_cited:
Mesbaul 
                                Alam & ors Vs. Bangladesh & ors (Kashefa Hussain, J) 19 SCOB [2024] HCD 14 Key Words: Sections 2(65), 4Ka of Bangladesh Labour Law, 
                                  2006; section 2(c) of Services (Reorganisation 
                                  & Conditions) Act-1975; dismissal; termination; 
                                  termination simpliciter; Section 14, 16 of the 
                                  সমবায় সমিতি আইন ও সমবায় সমিতি বিধিমালা; Sections 
                                  14 and 21 of the Co-operative Society Act- 2001; 
                                  Section 1(4)(ka) of the বাংলাদেশ শ্রম আইন-২০০৬
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 19 SCOB [2024] HCD 2] declared

facts_summary: In a Civil Revision petition (No. 1497 of 2021), Kazi Abdul Bakir challenged the continuation of a family case filed by his wife, Nur Taj Hossain Suchona, at the Family Court in Gazipur. The petitioner had initially brought a civil suit claiming that his marriage, which was solemnized in accordance with a kabinnama dated January 6, 2017, was invalid. The grounds for the invalidity were based on allegations that his wife had concealed the fact that she was already married at the time of their marriage, leading to a fraudulent enticement into the union. Subsequently, his wife had filed a family suit seeking Taka 10,00,000 in dower and maintenance. The petitioner had sought a stay on the family suit until the civil suit was resolved, but his request was denied by both the trial court and the appellate court. The petitioner's counsel argued before the Court that the two suits raised a common and fundamental question: whether a valid marriage existed between the parties. Therefore, according to Section 10 of the Code of Civil Procedure, the family suit should be stayed to avoid conflicting findings. In contrast, the wife's counsel contended that the Family Courts Ordinance, 1985, allowed the Family Court to incidentally determine the validity of a marriage whenever such a determination was essential to resolving claims for dower and maintenance. Upon reviewing the relevant statutes and judicial precedents, the Court held that despite the earlier filing of the civil suit, the Family Court retained jurisdiction to decide whether a valid marriage existed between the parties in order to resolve the ancillary claims. The Court further observed that multiple proceedings should be avoided and that effective relief could be granted in the family suit itself. As such, there was no compelling reason to stay the proceedings. Consequently, the Rule was discharged, the order of stay was vacated, and the Family Court was directed to proceed with the trial in a timely manner.
statutes_cited:
Kazi 
                                  Abdul Bakir Vs. Nur 
                                  Taj Hossain Suchona (Fatema Najib, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Keywords: Family court; Jurisdiction of family court to decide on the 
                                  validity of Kabinnama; Validity of marriage; Section 10 of CPC; Multiplicity of suits; Stay of family court proceedings pending civil 
                                  suit
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 11] directed to

facts_summary: Bangla Food and Beverage Limited lodged an appeal against the Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal's decision to dismiss its appeal due to an unpaid statutory amount, which was a prerequisite under Section 194 of the Customs Act, 1969. The condition for depositing the statutory amount was only applicable when goods were no longer under customs authority. Consequently, the Supreme Court ruled that the order issued by the tribunal was devoid of legal authority and thus null and void. As a result, the Court instructed the Tribunal to process the appeal within six months.
statutes_cited:
Bangla 
                                  Food and Beverage Limited Vs. The 
                                  Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal and 
                                  another (Ahmed Sohel, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Statutory Deposit; Custody of Goods; Custody 
                                  of Goods; Section 194 of the Customs Act, 1969
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] HCD 16] declared

facts_summary:
A government school instructor initiated proceedings against the respondent, who was a government primary school teacher. The school authority undertook an examination into allegations of misconduct levelled against the respondent but conducted the inquiry in the respondent's absence. A second summons to explain the allegations was issued to the respondent, but the inquiry report was not included, denying him the opportunity to mount a defence. Consequently, the authority terminated the respondent's employment. Dissatisfied with this outcome, the respondent filed an appeal with the Appellate authority, but this appeal remained unresolved within the stipulated timeframe of two months under the Administrative Tribunal Act.

The respondent thus proceeded to file a case with the Administrative Tribunal in Chittagong, which subsequently annulled the decision to dismiss the respondent from service. However, the Administrative Appellate Tribunal reversed this decision on appeal. The respondent then sought leave to appeal to the Appellate Division of the Supreme Court. In its subsequent judgment, the Appellate Division set aside the decision of the Administrative Appellate Tribunal, based in part on the grounds that the respondent was not afforded the chance to cross-examine witnesses or present evidence in his favour, as specified in Rule 10 of the Government Servants (Discipline and Appeal) Rules 1985.
statutes_cited:
Md. Syedul Abrar, son of late 
                                Ahmed Hossain Vs. Government of Bangladesh and others (Obaidul Hassan, J) 15 SCOB [2021] AD 102 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative 
                                  Appellate Tribunal; Departmental Inquiry Report; 
                                  Natural Justice; Disciplinary proceedings
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 15 SCOB [2021] AD 7] reversed

facts_summary: Petitions were submitted by various government department employees who are compensated by the hour, seeking to have their employment formalized within the revenue sector. These employees received court orders instructing their current employers to incorporate them as permanent staff. Subsequently, the government and other parties filed appeals to set aside these orders, but these appeals were rejected as being filed out of time. Following this, the government and others initiated review proceedings. In addressing these appeals, the Supreme Court ultimately determined that employees on an hourly wage who have worked for extended periods, such as 20 years or longer, might be considered permanent employees eligible for pensionary benefits. Drawing parallels with measures implemented by the Indian states to support employees on an hourly wage, the Supreme Court emphasized the need for the government to develop a policy to extend pensionary and other benefits to employees who have continuously served for 20 years or more without interruption.
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] AD 2] manual

facts_summary: A crucial inquiry arose in this matter regarding whether the respondent-petitioner, who had obtained the government's permission to undergo further training in Japan for six months and subsequently secured an additional leave of three months, remained a government employee despite being absent from service for seven years seven months and twenty-four days without the required authorization. The Bangladesh Service Rules, specifically Rule 34, 1st Part, necessitates that a government servant's status be terminated upon such prolonged absence without leave. Conversely, the respondent-petitioner was initially allowed to rejoin his position and worked for approximately one year and seven months. The Administrative Appellate Tribunal had concluded that by accepting the respondent-petitioner's rejoining, the Director General of Industry and Labour Wing effectively waived the government's right to reject his re-entry into service. However, the Appellate Division determined that the Director General lacked the authority to act under Rule 34 and, therefore, his decision was both void ab initio and devoid of lawful basis. Furthermore, the Court held that the principles of estoppel, waiver, and acquiescence do not apply to statutory provisions, thereby rendering the respondent-petitioner's re-entry into service for over a year insignificant in light of the clear statutory mandate in Rule 34.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                and another Vs. Sayed Mahabubul Karim ( Md. Nuruzzaman, J) 16 SCOB [2022] AD 46 Key Words: Rule 34, 1st Part of the Bangladesh Service 
                                  Rules; Doctrine of estoppels, waiver and acquiescence; 
                                  void ab intio
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] AD 7] manual

facts_summary: A former government official initiated a judicial review petition in relation to a service-related issue, obtaining a favorable preliminary ruling and a continuance of the status quo. In response, the government lodged an appeal with the Supreme Court, challenging the lower court's decision on the grounds that even after retirement, public servants must pursue administrative remedies through the Administrative Tribunal, as mandated by Section 4(3) of the Administrative Tribunal Act, 1980. The Supreme Court accepted the government's argument, finding that a prior decision (71 DLR (AD) 319) had incorrectly allowed writ petitions by retired public servants in service matters. The Supreme Court subsequently overruled its earlier decision, deeming it to be without legal basis, and vacated the rule issued by the lower court. The Court also clarified that, due to Article 111 of the Constitution, the lower court is obligated to adhere to the Supreme Court's decisions without question, and is only permitted to bring potential errors to the Chief Justice's attention. Furthermore, the Court held that subordinate courts lack jurisdiction to scrutinize the validity of a lower court's judgment, alleging it to be per incuriam, as only a court of equivalent stature may reevaluate and depart from such a decision should it be deemed incorrect.
statutes_cited:
Secretary, 
                                Posts & Telecom Div. & anr Vs. Shudangshu Shekhar & ors (Obaidul Hassan, 
                                  J) 18 SCOB [2023] AD 11 Key Words: Per incuriam; Section 4(3) of the Administrative 
                                  Tribunal Act, 1980; Article 111 of the Constitution; 
                                  maintainability of the writ petition by a retired 
                                  public servant in service matter
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 2] manual

facts_summary: The appellants successfully navigated the viva voce examination process, and the final outcome was made public on January 21, 2007. Subsequently, the Bangladesh Public Service Commission presented a list of 3,567 candidates, including the appellants, for consideration in various cadres. Meanwhile, certain allegations arose in the media regarding the 27th Bangladesh Civil Service examination, but these claims were disputed by the Commission. In an unprecedented move, the then interim government decided to void the results of the 27th BCS, which had already been announced by the Commission. Consequently, the Ministry of Establishment instructed the Commission to re-administer the viva voce examination to all successful candidates from the 27th BCS. The second examination took place, and the new results were published on September 23, 2008, recommending 3,239 candidates for appointment in various cadres, with the notable exclusion of 1,137 candidates, including the appellants, who had previously cleared the initial viva voce examination. A notification was subsequently issued in the official gazette on November 10, 2008. Several candidates, including the present appellants, who had earlier qualified in the first viva voce examination, filed writ petitions challenging both the second examination's results and the office order dated October 20, 2008. Following a hearing, the High Court Division issued judgments and orders on November 11, 2009, and January 26, 2010, directing the relevant parties to appoint the candidates, including the present appellants, who had been successful in the first viva voce examination, to their respective positions. The government challenged this decision by filing Civil Petition for Leave to Appeal Nos. 512, 513, and 514 of 2010. By judgment and order dated July 11, 2010, this Division set aside the High Court Division's judgment and order dated November 11, 2009. The present appellants, dissatisfied with this outcome, filed Civil Review Petition Nos. 197-199 of 2024, which was granted by this Division through an order dated November 7, 2024, giving rise to the present civil appeals, which were ultimately allowed by this judgment.
statutes_cited:
Mohammad 
                                  Sohel Rana & others Vs. Bangladesh 
                                  Public Service Commission (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Article 31,102 of the Constitution of Bangladesh; Setting aside judgment of the High Court Division 
                                  without granting leave; Principle of natural justice, audi alteram partem– 
                                  no man should be condemned unheard; Justice hurried, Justice buried;
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 3] manual

facts_summary: A petition was lodged with the Company Court under Section 43 of the Companies Act, 1994, to rectify the Chittagong Club Ltd's Members' Register, a private company incorporated without share capital. The petitioner sought a Court declaration that the General Committee's decision to suspend his membership was invalid and non-binding, thereby reinstating his name on the register. However, the High Court Division rejected the petition on maintainability grounds, reasoning that the dispute was purely of a civil nature and therefore required resolution through a civil Court. Concurrently, the Company Court levied a fine of 100,000 taka against the petitioner for squandering judicial time with an unsubstantiated claim.
statutes_cited:
Engr. Md. Anwar Hossen -VERSUS- Chittagong Club Ltd and others 15SCOB [2021] HCD 60 (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) Key Words: Golden rule of statutory interpretation; Section 
                                  43 and 44 of Companies Act, 1994; Rule 8 of 
                                  the Companies Rules, 2009; Section 2(1)(d), 
                                  Section 3(1), Section 43 and Section 233 of 
                                  the Companies Act, 1994
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 6] rejected

facts_summary: A debt judgment creditor, the respondent, had an execution case dismissed by the appellate court due to its time-barred nature. The Senior Assistant Judge initially denied the creditor's request to dismiss the case, citing a precedent from one of the High Courts of Pakistan established in 1998, as recorded in a referenced publication. However, the Appellate Division ultimately ruled that the cited precedent was inapplicable in the jurisdiction after March 25, 1971, and determined which prior decisions from the Dhaka High Court, Federal Court of Pakistan, Supreme Court of Pakistan, Calcutta High Court, Federal Court of India, and the Privy Council remained binding. The court then concluded that the execution proceedings had commenced beyond the three-year period specified in Article 182 of the Limitation Act, effectively rendering the case time-barred. Based on this finding, the appellate court dismissed the execution case.
statutes_cited:
Terab 
                                Ali & ors Vs. Syed Ullah & ors ( Md. Nuruzzaman, J) 17 SCOB [2023] AD 34 Key Words: Persuasive efficacy; interpretation of law; 
                                  Article 111 read with Article 149 of the Constitution 
                                  of Bangladesh, 1972
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] AD 2] affirmed

facts_summary: The Administrative Appellate Tribunal's determination that the penalty levied against the respondent was unlawful was contested. This was due to the fact that the Director General, who was in temporary charge of the institution, was not the appointing authority, and therefore had no authority to impose the penalty. When examining the relevant statutory provisions, the Appellate Division ultimately determined that an individual holding the post on an unlimited term basis is generally assumed to possess full authority over the institution's administration and finances. As a result, the Appellate Division overturned the findings of the Administrative Appellate Tribunal and reversed their verdict.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                & anr Vs. Nazrul Islam Biswas (M. Enayetur Rahim, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 119 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative Appellate 
                                  Tribunal; Current Charge; Section 4 (3) (b) 
                                  of the Government Servants (Discipline and Appeal) 
                                  Rules, 1985
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 19 SCOB [2024] AD 5] manual

facts_summary: The Minister of Rural Development and Co-operative Services issued a letter on 21 March 2005, pinpointing a specific location for the construction of the Pashapol Union Parishad Complex Building. The plaintiffs, however, argued that the government had previously designated an older site at Bariali Mouza for the Pashapol Union Parishad Office, where all necessary prerequisites for the building's construction were readily available. The plaintiffs claimed that disregarding this site rendered the government's letter null and void. In opposition, the defendants contested the plaintiffs' allegations, asserting that Pashapol serves as the central hub of the Union, boasting a hut, educational institutions, and a mosque. The defendants sought dismissal of the lawsuit. Initially, the trial court dismissed the suit, but the appellate court reversed the decision, decreeing the suit in favor of the plaintiffs. The High Court Division subsequently upheld this decision. The defendants then petitioned for a leave to appeal before the Appellate Division, which was granted. The defendants contended that the selection of the Union Parishad site is the government's prerogative and a matter of policy, rendering the plaintiffs without standing to challenge such a government decision. Conversely, the plaintiffs argued that the government's guidelines, as outlined in a circular dated 28 December 2005, were not adhered to in the impugned letter. The Appellate Division concurred with the view that shifting the site of the Union Parishad is an administrative decision and a matter of government policy. Consequently, the court held that the plaintiffs lacked standing to challenge the government's policy-making authority, and the appeal was allowed, resulting in the dismissal of the suit.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                  Obaidul Islam and others Vs. Md.Abdul 
                                  Mazid Mondal and others, (A.K.M. Asaduzzaman, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Declaration; selection of site of Union Parishad; 
                                  representative character suit; policy decision 
                                  of government; administrative decision
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 20 SCOB [2025] AD 6] appeal is allowed

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Primary and Mass Education and others
facts_summary: Teachers employed by the government in primary schools contested the application of the 2013 Service Conditions Rules, which resulted in a significant reduction of their credited service period and introduced unfair seniority standards that gave preference to newly appointed staff over those who had joined through nationalization, thereby impacting their career advancement and eligibility for certain benefits.
statutes_cited: অধিগ্রহণকৃত বেসরকারি প্রাথমিক বিশ্ববিদ্যালয়ের শিক্ষক (চাকুরি শর্তাবলী নির্ধারণ) বিধিমালা ২০১৩ Rules 2(Ga) and 9(1)

facts_summary: A candidate who had fully cleared all components of the BCS examination was formally proposed for employment by the Bangladesh Public Service Commission. However, the relevant government authority declined to appoint the candidate, failing to provide any legitimate or constitutional justification for such a decision.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Civil Service Recruitment Rules 1981 Rule 4(3)(A)(B); Constitution
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 16] Refusing appointment of BPSC-recommended candidate without valid cause declared unlawful and unconstitutional

respondent: Secretary, Ministry of Civil Aviation, Bangladesh and others
facts_summary: In a dispute involving Biman Bangladesh Airlines, an employee brought a challenge to the promotion selection process, arguing that it unfairly prioritized interview performance over the cumulative evaluation of service records compiled over a considerable period. This approach, the employee contended, introduced an element of unpredictability and rendered decisions vulnerable to potential illegality.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; ACR-based evaluation principles
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 15] Promotion solely by interview found unfair; petitioner deprived unlawfully and deserves retrospective promotion

facts_summary: A group of educators employed by government-run educational institutions lodged a dispute against the provisions of Nitimala 2012, contending they were overly restrictive and in contravention of constitutional rights.
