notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 309] Nitimala upheld as valid supplement to existing service rules; teachers already prohibited from coaching by their service rules

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Post Telecommunications and IT and others
facts_summary: A group of government employees, whose tenure spanned over a decade, brought a challenge to a revised executive directive impacting their potential regularization. Through consecutive extensions, the administration had fostered a reasonable expectation of long-term employment among these workers, thus forming a basis for their argument.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; Constitutional rights
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 11 SCOB [2019] HCD 46] Employees' legitimate expectation of absorption upheld after years of continuous service; executive order amendment found whimsical and beyond authority

facts_summary: Temporary personnel employed on a contingent basis by the government had been working for a prolonged period of 10 to 15 years. Despite their satisfactory performance, the administrative body had initially approved their permanent employment but ultimately neglected to implement the regularization of their positions.
statutes_cited: Legitimate expectation doctrine; service regularization principles
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 1] Long-serving temporary government employees cannot be denied expectation of regularization especially when their work was satisfactory

facts_summary: A medical professional, seeking promotion, found themselves disadvantaged due to a delayed evaluation process. As a result of this administrative failure, the individual unjustly lost accrued seniority rights despite not being responsible for the procedural lapse.
statutes_cited: Service promotion rules; principles of natural justice and equity
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 34] Seniority ordered to be restored from date of colleague's promotion; writ court as court of equity cannot allow unjust loss of seniority

appellant: Md. Yousuf Ali Akon and others
facts_summary: Appointees under BIWTA's probationary scheme for Master Pilots were assured of permanent integration upon successful completion of a one-year trial period, as explicitly stated in the BIWTA job advertisement. However, the respondents neglected to absorb the petitioners following their satisfactory tenure.
statutes_cited: Service regulations; legitimate expectation doctrine
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 66] Petitioners had legitimate expectation of absorption based on express promise in advertisement; BIWTA directed to permanently absorb them

facts_summary: In a case involving a career advancement from Steno-Typist to Stenographer, the issue at hand centered on whether accrued benefits from previous employment precluded an individual from qualifying for a new time scale upon promotion. Specifically, the question arose as to whether compensation and benefits received in the Steno-Typist position would prevent the employee from being eligible for time scales in their new role as Stenographer.
statutes_cited: Service rules; time scale regulations; promotion benefits
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 6 SCOB [2016] HCD 124] Promoted Stenographer entitled to fresh time scales in new post; previous time scales as Steno-Typist do not bar entitlement in promoted post

facts_summary: This court case concerned the scope of the Republic for Administrative Tribunal's authority over individuals, specifically those who are no longer actively employed. The issue at hand was whether the definition of "a person in the service of" encompasses individuals who have left their positions due to retirement, termination, removal, or discharge.
statutes_cited: Administrative Tribunal Act; Constitution
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 1] Retired, dismissed and discharged government servants fall within Administrative Tribunal jurisdiction for service-related grievances

appellant: Gazi A.K.M. Fazlul Haque and others
facts_summary: A dispute arose in the promotion procedures employed by the Privatization Commission. The central question at hand concerned the validity of using seniority as the sole determining factor for advancement and the influence that merit and the role of selection committees exercised in the promotion of Deputy Directors to the position of Director.
statutes_cited: Privatization Commission Officers and Employees Service Regulations 2002; Constitution Article 102
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 4 SCOB [2015] HCD 42] Both seniority and merit required for promotion; petitioners entitled to be considered for promotion under proper criteria including minimum 5 years service

facts_summary: A set of petitioners contested disciplinary measures imposed upon them by virtue of an alleged procedural irregularity, namely, the failure to serve a required show cause notice as stipulated within service regulations. Moreover, the petitioners also contended that the disciplinary proceedings were not disposed of by a mandatory three-member inquiry committee within the statutorily specified sixty-day timeframe.
statutes_cited: Government service disciplinary rules; show cause notice requirement Rule 5.4
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 2 SCOB [2015] HCD 99] Disciplinary action set aside; mandatory show cause notice and enquiry committee requirements under service rules were not followed

respondent: Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University (BSMMU) and others
facts_summary: At issue in this service law case was a disputed appointment at the Bangladesh Specialized Hospital and Medical University (BSMMU). The claimant brought the matter forward, contending that the selection committee had overstepped its authority in direct contravention of Article 31 of the Constitution. A core question revolved around whether an unlawful appointment could subsequently be legitimized through the passage of time and the rendering of service in the position in question.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 31; BSMMU appointment rules
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 14 SCOB [2020] HCD 3] Court held unlawful appointments cannot be validated by service; selection committee abuse of power is ground for setting aside interview result

facts_summary: A university Syndicate's determination was contested by the petitioners, which had significant implications for their entitlement. The court ultimately concluded that the Syndicate's actions were egregious, as they disregarded established tenets of natural justice by rendering a decision that was biased and failed to adhere to the prescribed legal framework.
statutes_cited: Principles of natural justice; administrative fairness doctrine
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 13 SCOB [2019] HCD 7] Syndicate order vitiated as gross violation of natural justice; failure to act fairly leads to arbitrariness which vitiates the impugned order

appellant: Prof. Dr. Muhammad Yunus and others
facts_summary: The petitioners disputed a charge sheet issued against them under Bangladesh Labour Act, 2006, in relation to their activities at Grameen Telecom. Labour Inspector identified non-compliance issues, which included a failure to convert probationary employees into permanent status, denial of paid annual leave, and the absence of a Labour Participation Fund and Labour Welfare Fund as mandated by law.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] HCD 275] Rule discharged; charge framing order upheld; limitation question is mixed question of law and fact requiring evidence; nonprofit company still liable for labor welfare contributions

facts_summary: A Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee contested a mandatory retirement enforced by the Chief Executive Officer/Managing Director without a preceding resolution from the board of directors. The entity's Articles of Association explicitly mandated that board resolutions were necessary for making decisions involving retirement. Notably, no authority existed to enable the CEO to make such determinations autonomously.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Biman Corporation Employee Regulations 1988; Bangladesh Biman Corporation Ordinance 1977; Articles of Association
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 108] Rule found merit; CEO's retirement order declared arbitrary and without jurisdiction for lack of board resolution and delegated authority

facts_summary: In a case involving Professor Yunus and Grameen Trust Corporation (GTC), the Labour Court was petitioned to review a charge framing order. The petitioners claimed that the management of GTC had breached several provisions of the Bangladesh Labour Act of 2006. Specifically, they alleged that GTC had failed to convert probationary employees to permanent positions, denied them annual leave, and neglected to establish a Labour Welfare Fund. During proceedings, Professor Yunus, a Nobel laureate, maintained that, as a non-involvement in financial matters, he had no responsibility in financial management.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 162] Rule discharged; charge framing upheld; Board of Directors responsible for GTC administration; nonprofit company not exempt from Labour Welfare Fund contribution

facts_summary: A Dhaka University academic, holding the position of Associate Professor, lodged an administrative challenge against her demotion to Assistant Professor, citing allegations of plagiarism as the basis for the downgrade. The University Syndicate had taken disciplinary action against her, but failed to adhere to the obligatory protocols, thereby depriving her of essential procedural safeguards.
statutes_cited: Enquiry Committee and Tribunal Regulations 1980 Regulation 7(a); Dhaka University Order 1973 Article 52; First Statutes of Dhaka University
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 182] Syndicate decision declared illegal; mandatory charge framing and opportunity to be heard not followed; decision vitiated by bias and malafide

facts_summary: A retired Additional District Judge contested the validity of Rule 300(a) of the Bangladesh Service Rules Part I. This rule resulted in the forfeiture of pension upon resignation from public service. The petitioner, having rendered 19 years of unbroken service, submitted a claim for pension following their resignation. However, the Comptroller and Auditor General rejected this application.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Service Rules Part I Rule 300; Constitution of Bangladesh Articles 27, 31
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 7] Rule 300(a) on pension forfeiture upon resignation declared ultra vires the Constitution; person who resigns is entitled to pension based on length of service

respondent: Sk. Md. Abdullah Faruque and others
facts_summary: In a recent service law case, the High Court Division made a ruling that nationalized college teachers should be absorbed as government college lecturers, based on the 2018 Absorption Rules. This decision was made despite the fact that the petitioners had not requested absorption under these specific rules and the writ petition was not confined to that particular term. The Appellate Division, however, disagreed with the HCD's approach, finding that they had overstepped the scope of the original writ petition as contained in the Rule Nisi.
statutes_cited: Constitution Article 102; Nationalized College Teachers Absorption Rules 2018; Supreme Court HCD Rules 1973 Chapter XIA
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 18 SCOB [2023] AD 6] HCD judgment set aside; court cannot grant relief beyond what was prayed for in the writ petition or the terms of the Rule Nisi

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Expatriates Welfare and others
facts_summary: A recruiting licence for emigration purposes issued to the applicant was revoked, and securities were forfeited. The revocation decision was made without affording the applicant a prior opportunity to present their case, thereby contravening the provisions of Section 14(1) of the Emigration Ordinance of 1982, which stipulates that a hearing must be conducted prior to any cancellation.
statutes_cited: Emigration Ordinance 1982 Section 14
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 12 SCOB [2019] HCD 231] Order of cancellation was bad in law; mandatory requirement of opportunity of being heard before cancellation of license was not followed

facts_summary: A group of petitioners objected to a disciplinary measure imposed by the Islamic University on them. Unfortunately, the institution neglected essential procedural steps – informing them of the accusations and proposed sanctions at the outset, and not allowing them the opportunity to respond before reaching a decision on their case.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102(2); principles of due process and natural justice
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 92] Non-compliance with due process elements is fatal to authority's subsequent action; both informing of charge and opportunity to reply are mandatory before disciplinary action

respondent: Chairman, First Labour Court, Dhaka and others
facts_summary: Bangladesh's Grameenphone has contested the Labour Court's authority to adjudicate workers involved in subcontracted employment arrangements. In this case, the court explored the nature of subcontracting agreements in the country, namely whether outsourced employees are directly employed by the service provider or are actually employed by the third-party contractor.
statutes_cited: Labour Act 2006; outsourcing contract principles
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 10 SCOB [2018] HCD 7] Outsourcing is a contract of services not employment; service recipient not the employer of persons engaged by the contractor; Labour Court jurisdiction does not extend to outsourced workers

respondent: Commissioner, Customs Bond Commissionerate and others
facts_summary: Following a prior agreement between the New Buildings Regulation (NBR) and a predecessor's proposal, the Bond Commissioner subsequently issued a show cause notice to the petitioner, whose license was now subject to NBR's pre-approval.  The later Bond Commissioner had no jurisdiction to revise the proposal that had been previously accepted by NBR. By concurrently requesting other bodies to suspend the license, the Bond Commissioner effectively issued a show cause notice with a predetermined outcome, thereby negating the purpose of any potential inquiry.
statutes_cited: Customs Bond regulations; natural justice principles; administrative fairness
notes: [Augmented copy 2 of 5 SCOB [2015] HCD 40] Closed-minded show cause notice declared unlawful; when authority has predetermined the outcome and simultaneously taken adverse action, the notice is a mere formality without legal validity

facts_summary:
A retired official from the country's public administration department filed a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) petition under Article 102(2) of the Constitution of the People's Republic of Bangladesh, seeking relief from the government's practice of extending the designation of its officers as Officers on Special Duty beyond the mandated 150-day period. This move allowed such officers to receive remuneration and benefits without providing any actual services, contravening the Constitution and prejudicing the interests of taxpayers.

As a result, a show-cause notice was issued, requiring the government to explain why it should not be restrained from continuing the practice of posting civil servants as Officers on Special Duty without assigning them any specific duties, as this was deemed to be in conflict with the Constitution.

The court ultimately decided in favor of the petitioner, declaring the ongoing practice unconstitutional and ordering it be discontinued. Consequently, the government's authority to extend the appointment of officers as Officers on Special Duty beyond the stipulated 150-day period was deemed unlawful.
statutes_cited:
M. Asafuddowlah -VERSUS- Government of Bangladesh 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 1 (Zubayer Rahman Chowdhury, J) Key Words: Article 20(2), 31, 88 and 102 (2) of the Constitution 
                                  of the People’s Republic of Bangladesh; Constitutionality 
                                  of posting Officers on Special Duty (OSD) for 
                                  unlimited period;
notes: [Augmented copy 3 of 15 SCOB [2021] HCD 1] declared

facts_summary: The petition was brought by a corporate leader and shareholder of a borrower company, which had obtained credit from a bank. However, due to the borrower company's persistent failure to settle its loan obligations, a significant default occurred. In an effort to restructure its loan, the corporate leader promised to make a portion of the down payment but ultimately did not fulfill this commitment. The bank subsequently issued a letter to the borrower company, requiring it to make the remaining down payment as per Bangladesh Bank's guidelines. Despite this, the corporate leader failed to take any measures to settle the outstanding payment. Upon this circumstance, the Bangladesh Bank served a notice to the corporate leader, demanding repayment of the loan within two months, with a warning that, if the repayment was not made, his position as a director of the Mercantile Bank would be vacated, as per Section 17 of the Banking Companies Act. The corporate leader then filed an application under Section 17(8) of the Banking Companies Act, 1991 and Section 43 of the Companies Act, 1994 to challenge the validity of his removal as a director of the Mercantile Bank and to protect his shares in the bank. The High Court Division, after a thorough examination of the relevant provisions of the Banking Companies Act, 1991 and the Artharin Adalat Ain, 2003, dismissed the petition, ruling that the directorship of any scheduled bank would be vacated if a director personally guaranteed a loan or assumed liability for another borrower. The court distinguished between the provisions of Section 17 of the Banking Companies Act and Section 5 of the Artharin Adalat Ain, and ordered an exemplary cost to be imposed on the corporate leader for abusing the judicial process.
notes: [Augmented copy 3 of 16 SCOB [2022] HCD 11] manual

facts_summary: The key issues in these appeals and civil petitions centered on the legality of the Bangladesh Telecommunication Regulatory Commission's (BTRC) demand for an additional spectrum fee based on the Market Competition Factor (MCF), the treatment of Value-Added Tax (VAT) by cellular mobile phone operators, and whether the BTRC was required to register under the VAT Act. The Appellate Division, disagreeing with some High Court Division findings, ruled that: (1) the BTRC's authority to alter charges and levies, as stated in the 2011 Cellular Mobile Phone Operator Regulatory and Licensing Guidelines, justified the MCF-based fee; (2) cellular mobile phone operators cannot retain VAT collected at source, but must pay it directly to the government exchequer, with spectrum and license fees-related VAT not being refundable; and (3) under the VAT Act 1991, the BTRC did not need to compulsorily register for VAT as per clause-7 (Ab¨vb¨ †mev)(N) of the second schedule.
statutes_cited:
Grameenphone 
                                Ltd & ors Vs. BTRC & Ors (Borhanuddin, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 96 Key Words: Sub-Section 3(Ga) of Section 3, 5(4), Sub-Section 
                                  4 of Section 6, Section 6 (4KaKa), Section 9(Uma), 
                                  15 (4) of the VAT Act, 1991; Rule 18(Ka) and 
                                  18(Uma) of the VAT Rules, 1991; Cellular Mobile 
                                  Phone Operator Regulatory and Licensing Guidelines, 
                                  2011; Market Competition Factor (MCF);
notes: manual

appellant: East West Property Development (Pvt.) Ltd. and another
facts_summary: The Petitioner contested the Deputy Commissioner's decision to cancel a property mutation, claiming that the underlying acquisition proceeding had been rendered invalid due to the repeal of the applicable legislation governing the acquisition. Notably, no official government order had been issued confirming the valid acquisition of the property in question.
statutes_cited: Emergency Requisition of Property Act 1948; স্থাবর সম্পত্তি অধিগ্রহণ ও হুকুমদখল আইন ২০১৭
notes: Property acquisition found invalid; no government decision existed and acquisition proceeding became non-est due to statutory repeal

facts_summary: The plaintiff sought to enforce a contractual obligation based on a reconveyance deed signed in 1963, which contained a clause allowing for repurchase until 1971. However, when P.O. No. 88 of 1972 was enacted, the repurchase condition had already elapsed, rendering the transaction a completed transaction beyond redemption.
statutes_cited: State Acquisition and Tenancy Act 1950 Sections 95 and 95A; P.O. No. 88 of 1972; P.O. No. 136 of 1972
notes: Plaintiff not entitled to relief as repurchase condition expired before Presidential Order; transaction was past and closed

facts_summary: A civil revision question arose in this case regarding the jurisdiction of a District Judge to entertain an application under section 7K of the Arbitration Act 2001 while passing an order under section 45 of the Evidence Act, 1872. Anamika Corporation Ltd., the petitioner, had initiated an arbitration proceeding under section 7K of the Arbitration Act, 2001, seeking an order to prevent the opposite parties from selling or transferring the scheduled property to any third party until the arbitration proceedings were completed. However, the opposite parties denied the existence of the agreement and filed an application under section 45 of the Evidence Act, 1872 for an expert handwriting examination of their signatures. The District Judge partially allowed this application, which led the petitioner to file a civil revision. The High Court Division ruled that the power to order an expert handwriting examination was granted only to the Arbitral Tribunal under section 17(ka) of the Arbitration Act, 2001, and the District Judge did not have the authority to pass an order under section 45 of the Evidence Act while issuing an ad-interim restraining order. As a result, the court held that the District Judge exceeded its limited powers in passing such an order and made the rule absolute.
statutes_cited:
Anamika 
                                Corp. Ltd. & ors Vs. Humayun M. Chowdhury & ors (Kashefa Hussain, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 119 Key Words: Order 7 Rule 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure,1908 
                                  ; Section 45 of the Evidence Act, 1872 ; Section 
                                  2, 7A, 10, 17(ka), 19(1)(4), and 32 of the Arbitration 
                                  Act, 2001; Expert opinion; Valid arbitration 
                                  agreement; Ad-interim injunction
notes: manual

respondent: Chairman, 1st Court of Settlement and another
facts_summary: A parcel of land was adjudged abandoned in accordance with the relevant provisions of Public Order 16 of 1972. At the critical juncture, neither the petitioners nor their predecessors-in-title were in physical possession of the property, only acquiring possession in 1984. The petitioners, who had secured a court decree compelling the specific performance of a contractual obligation, contested the initial determination that the land be considered abandoned property.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Abandoned Property Order P.O. 16 of 1972 Article 7; Abandoned Buildings Supplementary Provision Ordinance 1985 Section 5
notes: Abandoned property designation stands; petitioners not in possession at relevant time; decree for specific performance does not determine the abandoned character of property

facts_summary: In a High Court proceeding, the winning bidder of a vessel sold at auction sought an order from the High Court to allow the Marshall to hand over the vessel without requiring payment of customs duties and VAT. The bidder claimed that based on an earlier assurance from the Assistant Commissioner of Customs in Chattogram, he was not obligated to pay customs duties on the vessel. He also argued that, as the vessel was ordered to be sold for scrap, a Bill of Entry was necessary to claim customs duties, which was not present in this case. Despite these claims, the High Court Division held that, under sections 18, 23, 43, 44, 45, 51, 52, 53, and 79 of the Customs Act, as well as relevant provisions of the Import and Export Act, a foreign vessel entering Bangladesh is subject to customs duties, regardless of whether a Bill of Entry is submitted. As a result, the High Court ruled that the bidder must take possession of the vessel upon payment of applicable customs duties and government fees.
statutes_cited:
Chattogram 
                                Dry Dock Ltd Vs. M.T. Fadl-E-Rabbi & ors (Muhammad Khurshid Alam Sarkar, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 82 Key Words: Estoppel; Customs Duty; Bill of Entry; Imported 
                                  goods; Section 115 of the Evidence Act, 1872; 
                                  Section 18, 23, 43, 44, 45, 51, 52, 53 and 79 
                                  of the Customs Act, 1969; Section 2(c), 3(1) 
                                  and 3(2) of the Imports and Exports (Control) 
                                  Act, 1950
notes: rule is discharged

facts_summary: A certificate case was initiated against the respondent, who was ordered to repay a 250,000/- taka loan in monthly installments of Tk. 5,000/- from February 5, 2008. The Certificate Officer had directed the debtor to settle the debt in installments before seeking the Certificate Officer's assistance on February 1, 2016, to determine the outstanding balance. The respondent replied that Tk. 5,07,766.00 was owed. In response, the petitioned, challenging the validity and procedure of the certificate proceedings, sought High Court intervention and a temporary halt on the proceedings. The High Court Division found that section 5(5) of the Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 did not render the certificate proceeding as defective. However, the Certificate Officer's decision to begin the proceedings without verifying the claimed outstanding amount, and solely based on an improperly submitted request letter, was deemed unlawful. As a result, the certificate proceedings were nullified.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                Shahin Ikbal Vs. General Certificate Officer & ors (Md. Zakir Hossain, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 168 Key Words: Section 4, 6, 16 of the Public Demands Recovery 
                                  Act, 1913; Section 5(5) of the Artha Rin Adalat 
                                  Ain, 2003; Certificate proceeding; Certificate 
                                  Officer; Writ of certiorari; Calculation of 
                                  interest
notes: quashed

respondent: Judge, Artha Rin Adalat, Chattogram and others
facts_summary: The guarantor, seeking to void a civil detention order, filed a writ petition alleging that the order was issued improperly in an Artha Rin execution case. Specifically, the guarantor contended that the bank had failed to comply with the requirements under Section 34 when requesting the order. In its ruling, the court clarified the distinction between individuals subject to criminal prosecution (accused persons) and those who have defaulted on a financial obligation (judgment debtors), stating that the latter are not required to surrender to the court in the same manner as the former.
statutes_cited: Artha Rin Adalat Ain 2003 Sections 2, 4, 5, 6(1), 6(5), 34, 41, 44
notes: Rule made absolute; civil detention order quashed as bank did not file application under Section 34 with proper verification or affidavit

facts_summary: BPDB, along with the Petitioner, had previously signed power purchase agreements that included a binding arbitration provision under the Arbitration Act of 2001. However, the Petitioner sought to bypass the previously agreed-upon arbitration process by invoking the jurisdiction of the writ, aiming to instead interpret and contest the terms of the contracts.
statutes_cited: Arbitration Act 2001 Section 7; Constitution of Bangladesh Article 102
notes: Writ not maintainable; Section 7 of Arbitration Act restricts judicial intervention in matters covered by arbitration agreement; petitioner who signed contract with open eyes must use arbitration

facts_summary: The petitioner brought a challenge against his forced retirement from Biman Bangladesh Airlines Ltd by the CEO, claiming it was motivated by bad faith. In response, the CEO argued that as Managing Director, they have the authority to make such decisions, and the allegations of malice are unfounded. Additionally, the CEO cited the presumption in illustration (e) of Section 114 of the Evidence Act, which assumes official actions are lawful and regular, unless proven otherwise. The High Court Division ultimately ruled, based on its analysis of applicable laws and the evidence presented, that the CEO's order to retire the petitioner lacked the necessary board resolution and delegated authority, making it null and void, arbitrary, and motivated by malice.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                Mominul Islam Vs. Bangladesh & ors (Zafar Ahmed, J) 17 SCOB [2023] HCD 108 Key Words: Rule 5(Ka) of “h¡wm¡­cn ¢hj¡e L­f¡Ñ­lne LjÑQ¡l£ 
                                  (Ahpli¡a¡ J A¡e¤­a¡¢oL) ¢h¢dj¡m¡, 1988; Bangladesh 
                                  Biman Corporation Ordinance, 1977; principle 
                                  of approbation and reprobation ; Section 114 
                                  of the Evidence Act, 1872
notes: manual

respondent: Bangabandhu Sheikh Mujib Medical University (BSMMU) and others
facts_summary: The petitioner contested an appointment at Bangladesh Specialised Hospital and Medical University (BSMMU), contending that the selection committee overstepped its authority in contravention of Article 31 of the Constitution. A central issue in the case was whether an unlawful appointment could be validated through the passage of time and the rendering of service in the position.
statutes_cited: Constitution of Bangladesh Article 31; BSMMU appointment rules
notes: Court held unlawful appointments cannot be validated by service; selection committee abuse of power is ground for setting aside interview result

facts_summary: Bangla Food and Beverage Limited contested the Customs, Excise, and VAT Appellate Tribunal's dismissal of its appeal based on the company's failure to pay the required statutory amount under Section 194 of the Customs Act, 1969, while the goods in question remained in the custody of the customs authority. The Supreme Court ruled that Section 194's statutory deposit only applies once the goods have been released from customs control. Consequently, the Supreme Court deemed the tribunal's order void and without legal force, instructing the tribunal to reinstate Bangla Food and Beverage Limited's appeal and dispose of it within six months.
statutes_cited:
Bangla 
                                  Food and Beverage Limited Vs. The 
                                  Customs, Excise and VAT Appellate Tribunal and 
                                  another (Ahmed Sohel, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Key Words: Statutory Deposit; Custody of Goods; Custody 
                                  of Goods; Section 194 of the Customs Act, 1969
notes: declared

facts_summary: In this case, Civil Revision No. 1497 of 2021, Kazi Abdul Bakir, the petitioner, challenged the continuation of a family lawsuit filed by his wife, Nur Taj Hossain Suchona, at the Family Court in Gazipur. Initially, the petitioner had filed a civil lawsuit seeking a declaration that their marriage contract on January 6, 2017, was invalid due to alleged suppression of the wife's prior marriage and subsequent deceitful inducement into the current marriage. After this, the wife filed a family lawsuit for dowry of Tk. 1,000,000 and maintenance. The petitioner requested that the family lawsuit be put on hold until his civil lawsuit was settled, but this plea was rejected by both the lower court and the appellate court. During the revision, the petitioner's lawyer argued that both lawsuits raised a fundamental and identical question: whether a valid marriage existed between the couple, and that according to Section 10 of the Code of Civil Procedure, the family lawsuit should be stayed to prevent contradictory judgments. Conversely, the wife's counsel claimed that under the Family Courts Ordinance of 1985, the Family Court, as a special tribunal, was empowered to make incidental decisions about the validity of marriage if this was essential to determining the wife's claims for dowry and maintenance. After reviewing the relevant laws and court precedents, the Court ruled that despite the earlier institution of the civil lawsuit, the Family Court retained the authority to declare the validity of the marriage when deciding on ancillary claims. Emphasizing the principle that multiple proceedings should be avoided and effective relief can be granted in the family lawsuit itself, the Court found no compelling reason to halt the proceedings. Consequently, the rule was discharged, the order to stay was revoked, and the Family Court was directed to conduct the trial expeditiously.
statutes_cited:
Kazi 
                                  Abdul Bakir Vs. Nur 
                                  Taj Hossain Suchona (Fatema Najib, J) 20 SCOB [2025] HCD Keywords: Family court; Jurisdiction of family court to decide on the 
                                  validity of Kabinnama; Validity of marriage; Section 10 of CPC; Multiplicity of suits; Stay of family court proceedings pending civil 
                                  suit
notes: directed to

respondent: Government of Bangladesh, Ministry of Primary and Mass Education and others
facts_summary: Nationalized primary school teachers contested the 2013 Service Conditions Rules, which had a far-reaching impact by reducing their credited years of service by half and introducing biased promotion criteria that unfairly advantaged direct recruits over nationalized teachers, thereby affecting both their career advancement and pay scales.
statutes_cited: অধিগ্রহণকৃত বেসরকারি প্রাথমিক বিশ্ববিদ্যালয়ের শিক্ষক (চাকুরি শর্তাবলী নির্ধারণ) বিধিমালা ২০১৩ Rules 2(Ga) and 9(1)
notes: Rule 9(1) of 2013 Rules struck down as manifestly unreasonable and self-contradictory; nationalized teachers' seniority rights protected

facts_summary: A letter issued by the Senior Assistant Secretary of the Ministry of Rural Development and Co-operative on 21 March 2005 designated Pashapol as the location for the Pashapol Union Parishad Complex Building. The plaintiffs challenged this decision, claiming that the government had previously acknowledged the old site at Bariali Mouza as the site for the Pashapol Union Parishad Office, where all necessary construction requirements were met. The defendants disputed the plaintiffs' claims, arguing that Pashapol was the central location of the union, featuring essential facilities such as a mosque and educational institutions. The trial court dismissed the suit, but the Appellate Court later reversed this decision, decreeing the suit in favor of the plaintiffs. The High Court Division upheld this decision, prompting the defendants to seek leave to appeal to the Appellate Division. The defendants contended that the plaintiffs lacked the right to challenge the government's policy decision on selecting the site for the Union Parishad Complex Building. However, the respondents argued that the government had failed to follow its own guidelines, as outlined in a circular dated 28 December 2005, when selecting the site. The Appellate Division ultimately ruled that the site selection was a policy matter and an administrative decision, beyond the plaintiffs' locus standi, and thereby allowed the defendants' appeal and dismissed the suit.
statutes_cited:
Md. 
                                  Obaidul Islam and others Vs. Md.Abdul 
                                  Mazid Mondal and others, (A.K.M. Asaduzzaman, J) 20 SCOB [2025] AD Key Words: Declaration; selection of site of Union Parishad; 
                                  representative character suit; policy decision 
                                  of government; administrative decision
notes: appeal is allowed

facts_summary: A serving Additional District Judge contested the validity of Rule 300(a) of Bangladesh Service Rules Part I, arguing that the rule's stipulation of forfeiting pension upon resignation from service was unjust. The petitioner, who had accumulated 19 years of unbroken and unblemished service, submitted an application for pension subsequent to their resignation; however, this application was subsequently turned down by the Comptroller and Auditor General.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Service Rules Part I Rule 300; Constitution of Bangladesh Articles 27, 31
notes: Rule 300(a) on pension forfeiture upon resignation declared ultra vires the Constitution; person who resigns is entitled to pension based on length of service

facts_summary: A Biman Bangladesh Airlines employee contested the imposition of compulsory retirement by the CEO/Managing Director, citing that no board of directors' resolution had been obtained, as mandated by the Articles of Association. This decision was taken without any explicit delegated authority allowing the CEO to unilaterally make such a call.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Biman Corporation Employee Regulations 1988; Bangladesh Biman Corporation Ordinance 1977; Articles of Association
notes: Rule found merit; CEO's retirement order declared arbitrary and without jurisdiction for lack of board resolution and delegated authority

appellant: Md. Yousuf Ali Akon and others
facts_summary: Petitioners were initially hired as Master Pilots on a probationary basis, with the Bangladesh Inland Water Transport Authority (BIWTA) having publicly guaranteed in a job advertisement that they would be offered permanent positions upon completion of their one-year probationary period provided they delivered satisfactory service.
statutes_cited: Service regulations; legitimate expectation doctrine
notes: Petitioners had legitimate expectation of absorption based on express promise in advertisement; BIWTA directed to permanently absorb them

facts_summary: The Administrative Appellate Tribunal's determination that the penalty levied on the respondent was unlawful due to the Director General's lack of authority as the appointing authority at the time of imposition, was contested. Upon examining the pertinent statutory provisions, the Appellate Division ruled that the delegation of the current charge for an indefinite period granted the Director General all administrative and financial authority of the institution, thereby affirming the Director General's authority to impose the penalty. As a result, the Appellate Division overturned the judgment of the Administrative Appellate Tribunal.
statutes_cited:
Bangladesh 
                                & anr Vs. Nazrul Islam Biswas (M. Enayetur Rahim, J) 19 SCOB [2024] AD 119 Key Words: Administrative Tribunal; Administrative Appellate 
                                  Tribunal; Current Charge; Section 4 (3) (b) 
                                  of the Government Servants (Discipline and Appeal) 
                                  Rules, 1985
notes: manual

facts_summary: A senior academic staff member at Dhaka University contested her demotion from the position of Associate Professor to Assistant Professor, citing alleged plagiarism as the reason. The University Syndicate imposed the demotion without adhering to essential procedural requirements, including failing to issue a formal charge, furnish the inquiry report, and provide a substantial opportunity for the staff member to present her defense.
statutes_cited: Enquiry Committee and Tribunal Regulations 1980 Regulation 7(a); Dhaka University Order 1973 Article 52; First Statutes of Dhaka University
notes: Syndicate decision declared illegal; mandatory charge framing and opportunity to be heard not followed; decision vitiated by bias and malafide

facts_summary: A pivotal question arose in this case regarding the maintainability of a writ petition against Milk Vita. The High Court Division ultimately determined that Milk Vita constitutes a public body rather than a private entity, thereby making the writ maintainable. Furthermore, the court held that the petitioners do not qualify as workers, thus precluding them from pursuing their matter before the Labor Court. Lastly, the court ruled that the impugned memos were not issued in accordance with the law, and, as such, the court declared them to be devoid of lawful authority. Consequently, the court instructed the concerned authority to move against the petitioners under clause 8.06 of the চাকুরি বিধি ও নিয়োগ নীতিমালা ২০০৮ (সংশোধিত ২০০৯) and to settle the matter in accordance with the law.
statutes_cited:
Mesbaul 
                                Alam & ors Vs. Bangladesh & ors (Kashefa Hussain, J) 19 SCOB [2024] HCD 14 Key Words: Sections 2(65), 4Ka of Bangladesh Labour Law, 
                                  2006; section 2(c) of Services (Reorganisation 
                                  & Conditions) Act-1975; dismissal; termination; 
                                  termination simpliciter; Section 14, 16 of the 
                                  সমবায় সমিতি আইন ও সমবায় সমিতি বিধিমালা; Sections 
                                  14 and 21 of the Co-operative Society Act- 2001; 
                                  Section 1(4)(ka) of the বাংলাদেশ শ্রম আইন-২০০৬
notes: declared

appellant: Prof. Dr. Muhammad Yunus and others
facts_summary: Petitioners contested a charge sheet issued against them under the Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 at Grameen Telecom. Labour Inspector discovered infractions including the failure to convert temporary workers to permanent after their probationary periods, the denial of paid annual leave, and the non-establishment of a Labour Participation Fund and a Labour Welfare Fund.
statutes_cited: Bangladesh Labour Act 2006 Sections 303(Uma), 307, 117, 314; Labour Welfare Foundation Law 2006
notes: Rule discharged; charge framing order upheld; limitation question is mixed question of law and fact requiring evidence; nonprofit company still liable for labor welfare contributions
